title: A Different Pond
author: Bao Phi
heads_up: Brief reference to war and family members who did not survive. Handled with restraint, not graphic.
orbital_description: Bao Phi wrote this from his own childhood: a Vietnamese refugee father who fished not for sport but for dinner. Thi Bui's watercolors hold two geographies simultaneously, the Minnesota pond and the Vietnamese landscape the father carries inside him. The child absorbs immigration, food economics, freshwater ecology, and intergenerational memory in a single quiet morning. This is not a sad book. It is a serious one.
hook: A father and son go fishing before dawn. The pond is in Minneapolis. The memory is in Vietnam.

title: A Drop of Water
author: Walter Wick
orbital_description: Photography book, chemistry book, weather book, and art book simultaneously. Every page invites an experiment.
hook: Macro photographs so close to water you can see it think about whether to freeze, boil, or float.

title: A Flea for Justice: Marian Wright Edelman Stands Up for Change
author: Valerie Bolling
orbital_description: Fall 2025 pub. Opens the history of children's advocacy as a distinct political movement through Marian Wright Edelman's biography, the civil rights movement's unfinished work in addressing economic inequality, the geography of poverty across American communities in the 1960s-1970s, and the concept of making people in power uncomfortable as a tool for justice.
hook: Marian Wright Edelman founded the Children's Defense Fund after realizing the civil rights movement had not yet reached the most vulnerable Americans — the children living in poverty.

title: A Flicker of Hope: A Story of Migration
author: Cynthia Harmony
heads_up: PUB YEAR NEEDS VERIFICATION — Charlotte Huck Honor 2025 = likely 2024 pub.
orbital_description: Charlotte Huck Honor 2025. Devon Holzwarth opens the biology of bird migration through the specific behavioral and physiological mechanisms of migratory birds alongside the social-emotional experience of a refugee family in motion. The parallel structure makes both journeys feel universal. NOTE: May be 2024 pub — verify.
hook: A child's migration story is told in parallel with a bird's migration — two creatures crossing uncertain distances toward something they can't yet see.

title: A House for Hermit Crab
author: Eric Carle
orbital_description: Eric Carle walks through the calendar year with a hermit crab who decorates his shell month by month: a sea anemone in February, a starfish in March, coral in April. Each addition teaches a marine organism and a symbiotic relationship. The child absorbs the twelve-month calendar, ocean biology, the concept of symbiosis, and the emotional arc of outgrowing something you built. The tissue-paper collage style carries texture and color theory on every page.
hook: Every month, Hermit Crab adds something new to his shell. By December, he has outgrown it entirely.

title: A Line Can Go Anywhere: The Brilliant, Resilient Life of Artist Ruth Asawa
author: Caroline McAlister
heads_up: Discusses Japanese American incarceration; preview before reading.
orbital_description: PW starred. Opens Japanese American incarceration through the specific experience of a child who responded to confinement by learning to make art, the materials science of wire sculpture, the art history of wire and line as expressive form, and the biography of a woman who turned the geometry she learned behind barbed wire into internationally celebrated public sculpture.
hook: Ruth Asawa spent her childhood in a Japanese American incarceration camp, where she learned to draw — and became one of the most innovative sculptors of the 20th century.

title: A Most Perilous World: The True Story of the Young Abolitionists and Their Crusade Against Slavery
author: Kristina R. Gaddy
orbital_description: SLJ 2025 Stars So Far starred review. Opens the history of American abolitionism through the childhood and young adulthood of the children of Garrison, Weld, Grimké, and Douglass, the geography of the antislavery movement across New England and the North, and the experience of growing up with radical parents in a society where their cause was considered extremist.
hook: Four children of four famous abolitionists grew up inside the movement to end slavery — and their interwoven biographies open the inner world of America's most important social reform movement.

title: A Rover's Story
author: Jasmine Warga
orbital_description: The book opens Mars geography and geology, actual NASA rover technology and mission design, the physics of space travel, and the philosophical question of whether machines can develop something like feeling. The dual narrative between the rover and a girl whose father works on the mission grounds the science in human stakes.
hook: A Mars rover learns what it means to be human from the humans who built him — and asks whether empathy can be programmed.

title: A Sea of Lemon Trees: The Corrido of Roberto Alvarez
author: María Dolores Águila
orbital_description: The 2026 Newbery Honor and Belpré Author Honor Book opens the history of anti-Mexican sentiment in 1930s California, the Lemon Grove Incident as the first successful legal challenge to school segregation in American history, the geography of California's agricultural valleys, and the economics of citrus farming.
hook: The Lemon Grove Incident of 1931 was the first successful desegregation case in American history — and it started with one Mexican American boy refusing to go to the separate school.

title: A Seed Grows
author: Antoinette Portis
orbital_description: The book opens the botanical life cycle of a flowering plant through precise sequential illustration, the chemistry of germination and photosynthesis, and the ecology of pollination. The fold-out sunflower spread makes the scale visceral. The back matter extends into specific plant science.
hook: A single seed's complete journey from soil to sunflower to seed again — with a fold-out at the end that is the full-grown plant itself.

title: A Window into the Ocean Twilight Zone
author: Michelle Cusolito
orbital_description: The book opens deep ocean science through the twilight zone (200-1000 meters) as a specific ecological layer, the technology required to study it including remotely operated vehicles and midwater trawls, and the 24-day expedition structure that organizes the narrative. Every discovery is real data from actual scientific missions.
hook: The ocean twilight zone begins at 200 meters and holds 10 billion tons of life — and almost no one has seen it.

title: A-Ztec: A Bilingual Alphabet Book
author: Emmanuel Valtierra
orbital_description: The 2026 Belpré Illustration Honor Book opens the linguistics of Mesoamerican language families including Nahuatl and Mayan, the history of pre-Columbian indigenous civilizations, and the concept of bilingualism as intellectual expansion rather than deficit. Each letter opens a word from a different indigenous language with its cultural context.
hook: The alphabet told in Spanish, Nahuatl, Mayan, and other Mesoamerican languages — a reminder that American history goes 3,000 years deeper than the English alphabet.

title: Abolition and the African American Story
author: Patricia Williams Dockery
orbital_description: SLJ Best Nonfiction MG/HS 2025. Part of the 'Race to the Truth' nonfiction series. Opens the full arc of American abolitionism from the 1600s through Reconstruction, with particular attention to Black abolitionists who are systematically left out of the standard narrative, the geography of the Underground Railroad, and the economics of slavery as an institution.
hook: A sweeping history of the Black abolitionist movement, the Civil War, and Reconstruction — told through names and stories that most American classrooms never teach.

title: Across So Many Seas
author: Ruth Behar
orbital_description: The Sydney Taylor Honor and multiple-starred-review novel opens Sephardic Jewish diaspora history from the 1492 Expulsion from Spain through five centuries of migration across the Mediterranean, Ottoman Empire, Caribbean, and Americas. Each girl's chapter opens a different moment in history through the intimate lens of a family.
hook: Five girls, five centuries, five countries — and one Sephardic Jewish family's journey across 500 years of diaspora history from Spain to Turkey to Cuba to the United States.

title: Actual Size
author: Steve Jenkins
orbital_description: Animals printed at their true scale. Biology, measurement, proportion, estimation, and cut-paper art.
hook: A gorilla's hand is this big. Open the page. It is actual size.

title: Afloat
author: Kirli Saunders
orbital_description: The 2026 ALSC Notable Book opens flood science and water management, the concept of traditional ecological knowledge as a legitimate and often superior problem-solving framework, and the social architecture of a community that listens to its elders. Saunders is an Aboriginal Australian author writing across cultures about a universal experience.
hook: A community faces a flood — and the Elder's indigenous knowledge provides the solution no one else thought of.

title: After the Rain
author: Eleanor Spicer Rice
orbital_description: PW Spring 2026 preview. Eleanor Spicer Rice (Dr. Eleanor's Book of Common Ants, SLJ starred) opens soil ecology through the post-rain cascade: how water infiltrates soil, the chemistry of petrichor (the smell of rain), the role of earthworms in soil aeration, and the behavior of insects and amphibians triggered by rainfall. Illustrated science for young naturalists.
hook: What happens in the natural world in the minutes and hours after rain falls? The answer involves chemistry, soil biology, and the most underrated creatures on Earth.

title: Aggie and the Ghost
author: Matthew Forsythe
orbital_description: The 2026 ALSC Notable Book opens conflict resolution through the specific mechanics of establishing shared rules in a shared space. The ghost is a genuine character with genuine grievances; the negotiation between Aggie and the ghost models structured compromise with consequences.
hook: Aggie's new house comes with a ghost — and they must negotiate house rules before they can coexist.

title: Al Capone Does My Shirts
author: Gennifer Choldenko
orbital_description: The book opens 1930s San Francisco Bay geography, Alcatraz as a functioning federal prison, autism before it was called autism, and the economics of a family prioritizing a disabled member's care. Moose's scheme is both clever and heartbreaking.
hook: A twelve-year-old boy moves to Alcatraz Island when his father gets a job there — and uses Al Capone to try to help his autistic sister.

title: Alberto Salas Plays Paka Paka con la Papa
author: Sara Andrea Fajardo
orbital_description: The 2026 Sibert Medal winner opens the biology of potato biodiversity including the thousands of potato varieties native to Peru, the geography of the Andes as the potato's place of origin, the ecology of climate change threatening wild crops, and the science of genetic preservation. Martinez-Neal's Caldecott Honor-level art brings the Andes to life.
hook: Alberto Salas hunts for wild potatoes in the Andes Mountains to preserve biodiversity before they go extinct — and potato conservation is more urgent than you think.

title: Alexander and the Terrible Horrible No Good Very Bad Day
author: Judith Viorst
orbital_description: The book validates emotional escalation and models perspective-taking, introducing the concept that suffering is universal and temporary. The Australian refrain carries its own quiet geography lesson.
hook: Alexander's day is a masterclass in catastrophizing — and the punchline is that some days are just bad, even in Australia.

title: Almost American Girl
author: Robin Ha
orbital_description: Opens the history and cultural significance of K-pop and manhwa (Korean comics), the geography of South Korea and the cultural landscape of Seoul, the social history of Korean Americans in the South through Robin's mother's experience, and the concept of fandom as a universal language that crosses cultural barriers.
hook: Robin moves from Seoul to Alabama knowing nobody — and discovers an American comic book club that shares her same love of comics, just in a different language.

title: Almost Underwear: How a Piece of Cloth Traveled from Kitty Hawk to the Moon and Mars
author: Jonathan Roth
orbital_description: ALSC 2026 Notable Recordings (implies 2025 pub). Opens materials science through the history of textile technology in aerospace engineering, from the fabric-covered wings of the Wright Flyer to the thermal quilts of the Space Shuttle to the materials of the Mars rovers. An engineering history told through the thread connecting Kitty Hawk to space.
hook: The outer material of the Space Shuttle wasn't metal or glass — it was a specialized fabric quilted like a blanket, two inches thick. And one of its ancestors flew on the Wright Brothers' first airplane at Kitty Hawk.

title: Amari and the Night Brothers
author: B.B. Alston
orbital_description: Coretta Scott King Author Honor. Opens the fantasy tradition of a secret institution hidden inside the ordinary world through the lens of a Black girl fighting both the supernatural and institutional barriers simultaneously. The mystery of the brother's disappearance runs through every page.
hook: Amari discovers a secret supernatural agency — and a file with her missing brother's name — and must prove she belongs there despite everyone insisting she doesn't.

title: Amelia Bedelia
author: Peggy Parish
orbital_description: Every misunderstanding is a genuine linguistic ambiguity: 'draw the drapes,' 'dust the furniture,' 'change the towels.' The book teaches that language depends on shared context — and that lemon pie fixes almost everything.
hook: Amelia Bedelia takes every instruction she's given completely literally — and the chaos is a perfect lesson in the ambiguity of language.

title: American Murderer: The Parasite That Haunted the South
author: Gail Jarrow
orbital_description: The NPR 2022 Books We Love selection opens the biology of hookworm infestation including the parasite's lifecycle, the public health investigation that identified it, the economics of poverty that enabled the epidemic to persist, and the politics that blocked the cure. Science and social history are inseparable.
hook: Millions of people in the American South were chronically sick, exhausted, and dismissed as lazy — and the cause was a microscopic parasite that entered through bare feet.

title: American Spirits: The Famous Fox Sisters and the Mysterious Fad That Haunted a Nation
author: Barb Rosenstock
orbital_description: Confirmed April 15, 2025, ages 12-18. Barb Rosenstock opens the social history of the American spiritualism movement of the 19th century through the Fox sisters' specific fraud and the science of what made it so believable, the Civil War-era grief that made millions desperate to contact the dead, and the psychology of mass belief as a historical phenomenon.
hook: Two sisters in 1848 New York claimed they could communicate with spirits — and launched a national spiritualism craze that swept America for 40 years, attracting presidents and scientists and eventually destroying the sisters themselves.

title: An American Plague: The True and Terrifying Story of the Yellow Fever Epidemic of 1793
author: Jim Murphy
heads_up: Contains descriptions of disease symptoms.
orbital_description: Newbery Honor, Sibert Medal 2004. Opens the epidemiology of yellow fever including how it is transmitted (Aedes aegypti mosquitoes, then unknown) and how it kills, the history of Philadelphia as the political center of the new republic, and the social history of class and race through who fled and who stayed.
hook: In 1793, yellow fever swept through Philadelphia — the nation's capital — killing 5,000 people in three months and destroying the city's functioning government.

title: An Immense World (Young Readers Edition): How Animals Sense Earth's Amazing Secrets
author: Ed Yong
orbital_description: SLJ 2025 Stars So Far starred review. Ed Yong (Pulitzer Prize winner) adapted his acclaimed adult book for middle grade readers. Opens the biology of sensory systems across the animal kingdom including the concept of 'umwelt' — the species-specific sensory world each animal inhabits — magnetoreception, electroreception, lateral lines, and infrared detection.
hook: Every animal lives in its own sensory world — and a dog's sense of smell, a bat's echolocation, and an electric eel's electroreception are not just 'better' versions of human senses but completely different realities.

title: And Then, Boom!
author: Lisa Fipps
heads_up: PUB YEAR NEEDS VERIFICATION — Charlotte Huck Honor 2025 = likely 2024 pub.
orbital_description: Charlotte Huck Honor 2025. Lisa Fipps (Starfish, ALSC Notable) opens the social-emotional experience of a middle schooler navigating body image through swimming — the specific physics of what happens to a body in water — and the literary technique of the novel in verse as a container for feelings too large for prose. NOTE: May be 2024 pub — verify.
hook: A girl finds her voice in a swimming pool and a poem — from the author of Starfish.

title: Anything
author: Rebecca Stead
orbital_description: Kirkus Best 2025 opens the mathematical concept of infinity through the conceit of unlimited wishes, the philosophical idea of possibility as a landscape, and the social-emotional architecture of childhood wonder as a cognitive resource. Zhang's illustration of 'how many anythings' is the kind of spread children return to for years.
hook: How many anythings can you wish for? The spread that answers this question is one of the most visually abundant pages in recent children's books.

title: At Last She Stood: How Joey Guerrero Spied, Survived, and Fought for Freedom
author: Erin Entrada Kelly
orbital_description: SLJ 2025 Stars So Far starred review ('belongs in every library'). Erin Entrada Kelly (Hello, Universe; Newbery Medal) opens the Japanese occupation of the Philippines in WWII, the biology and history of leprosy, the geography of Manila under occupation, and the specific courage of a woman who used the stigma of disease as a tool of resistance.
hook: Joey Guerrero was a Filipina woman with leprosy who spied for the Americans during the Japanese occupation of the Philippines — and her condition made her invisible to guards who feared contagion.

title: At the Window
author: Hope Lim
orbital_description: The ALSC 2026 Notable Book opens the social geography of urban apartment living, the way careful observation builds connection across distance, and the emotional sequence of friendship, loss, and reorientation. Qin Leng's luminous illustrations make window light a character in its own right.
hook: Two neighbors connect through their apartment windows — and when one window goes dark, the child learns to open her own.

title: Awe!
author: Jilanne Hoffman
orbital_description: Pub March 3, 2026, Scholastic. Jilanne Hoffman opens the emerging science of awe as a distinct emotion with measurable physiological effects including reduced self-focus, increased generosity, and improved mental health, through a picture book designed to provoke the actual experience of awe rather than just describe it. The second-person format pulls children into moments of genuine wonder.
hook: Awe is not just a feeling — it's a scientifically studied emotion that shrinks your sense of self, connects you to something larger, and makes you measurably kinder. This book opens that emotion for the youngest readers.

title: Barbed Wire Between Us
author: Mia Wenjen
heads_up: Discusses detention and historical injustice; discuss before reading.
orbital_description: Kirkus Spring 2026 preview pick. Opens the history of Japanese American incarceration through the lens of a direct comparison to contemporary immigration detention, the geography of both historical and modern detention sites, and the concept of parallel injustice as a pattern in American history.
hook: Japanese Americans incarcerated during WWII and young Latinx immigrants detained at the US-Mexico border today — two histories of children behind fences, one book.

title: Bartleby
author: Matt Phelan
orbital_description: Multiple starred reviews from MacKids 2026. A new picture book from the New York Times bestselling author and illustrator, described as 'a modern classic for fans of The Rabbit Listened.' Opens identity formation and the social pressure to conform, modeled through a polar bear who refuses to change his essential nature under social pressure.
hook: Bartleby is a dapper polar bear who knows exactly who he is — and the book is a modern classic for children who feel the pressure to be someone different.

title: Baseball's Shining Season: America's Pastime on the Brink of War
author: Martin W. Sandler and Craig Sandler
orbital_description: Kirkus Spring 2026 Preview. From the author of Shipwrecked! and the Sibert Honor team, opens the 1941 baseball season through detailed player statistics and the history behind them, the pre-Pearl Harbor geopolitical atmosphere of American anxiety, the sociology of sports as communal coping, and the specific geography of baseball stadiums as American institutions.
hook: 1941: Joe DiMaggio hit safely in 56 consecutive games, Ted Williams batted .406, and Americans were terrified about a war they weren't yet in — and baseball was the only escape.

title: Bat and the Business of Ferrets
author: Elana K. Arnold
orbital_description: 2026 Schneider Family Book Award (disability representation). Elana K. Arnold (A Boy Called Bat series) continues opening the world through Bat's neurodivergent perspective, ferret biology, and the social dynamics of a neighborhood seen through a child who processes it differently. NOTE: Likely pub 2025 — verify before placing in 2026 shelf.
hook: Bat's ferret is central to a neighborhood mystery — and Bat, who is on the autism spectrum, approaches the mystery with the same methodical logic he applies to everything.

title: Beautiful Black Boy
author: Sophia Robinson
orbital_description: April 2026 release from Scholastic. Each spread pairs affirmational language ('You are strong. You can do anything you set your mind to') with rich illustration. The visual design draws from West African textile and pattern traditions. Every page is crafted for read-aloud memorization.
hook: A joyful, affirmational celebration of Black boyhood — every spread an explosion of color and pride, with words designed to live in a child's head.

title: Beautiful Oops
author: Barney Saltzberg
orbital_description: Each page demonstrates creative transformation: a torn page becomes a bird's beak, a smudge becomes a bear. The book reframes mistakes as material rather than failure, which is the foundational premise of every design field.
hook: Every rip, spill, and smudge becomes a new creation — the most joyful case for growth mindset ever bound between covers.

title: Bee Dance
author: Rick Chrustowski
orbital_description: Rick Chrustowski follows a single scout bee from hive to flower field and back. The core revelation is the waggle dance: bees communicate distance and direction through choreography, using the sun's angle as a reference point. The child absorbs animal communication, angular measurement, solar navigation, and colony organization through one bee's afternoon. The illustrations are scientifically precise enough to teach pollination mechanics while staying visually warm. After this one, your child will watch bees differently.
hook: A honeybee finds flowers. Then she goes home and dances the directions.

title: Before Colors
author: Javier Saez Castan
orbital_description: 2025 ALSC 2026 Notable (confirmed 2025 pub). Opens the physics of light and color perception including the electromagnetic spectrum, the role of photoreceptors in the human eye, and the philosophical question of whether color exists in the world or only in the mind that perceives it. The visual design enacts the concept: the book starts without color and gains it gradually.
hook: Before there were colors, there was light — and this picture book is a physics lesson about perception disguised as a bedtime question.

title: Before Colors: Where Pigments and Dyes Come From
author: Annette Bay Pimentel
orbital_description: The book opens chemistry through the science of light absorption and pigment molecules, art history through the pigments behind specific historical paintings, natural history through the plants and animals that produce color, and trade history through the economics of rare pigments like Tyrian purple and lapis lazuli.
hook: Every color you've ever used started as something else — a rock, a plant, an insect, a shell — and this book shows exactly where.

title: Before the Ships: The Birth of Black Excellence in American Aviation
author: Maisha Oso
heads_up: PUB YEAR NEEDS VERIFICATION.
orbital_description: ALSC 2026 Notable Recordings (implies 2025 book pub). Opens the history of Black Americans in aviation from the earliest aviators through the Tuskegee Airmen, the geography of the segregated South where most of this history was made despite prohibition, and the physics of early flight through the specific aircraft these pilots flew. PUB YEAR NEEDS VERIFICATION.
hook: Before the Tuskegee Airmen, there was Bessie Coleman — and before Bessie Coleman, there were the dreamers who built the foundation.

title: Ben Franklin's Almanac
author: Candace Fleming
orbital_description: The almanac format matches Franklin's own Poor Richard's format, teaching the genre while delivering the content. Electricity, bifocals, the Franklin stove, the library, the postal system — and the man who owned enslaved people while writing about liberty.
hook: Benjamin Franklin's life in scrapbook form — his inventions, his wit, his politics, and his contradictions all on the same page.

title: Benny on the Case
author: Wesley King
orbital_description: The ALSC 2026 Notable Book opens Mosaic Down syndrome as a lived cognitive experience rather than a plot device, retirement home community as a rich social ecosystem, and the mystery structure through a protagonist whose different ways of noticing make him a better detective than the neurotypical adults around him.
hook: Benny is 11, has Mosaic Down syndrome, lives in a retirement home run by his mother, and is the only one qualified to solve the mystery of the missing belongings.

title: Big
author: Vashti Harrison
orbital_description: The book opens the psychology of body image and how language shapes identity over a lifetime, alongside the neuroscience of emotional memory. Harrison's pastel dreamscapes earned the 2024 Caldecott Medal. The visual language shifts from warm and soft to hard and isolating as the girl encounters cruelty, then returns to warmth when she reclaims herself.
hook: A baby girl grows up hearing the word 'big' used as a wound — and eventually turns it into a crown.

title: Blast Off!: The Story of Mary Sherman Morgan, America's First Female Rocket Scientist
author: Suzanne Slade
orbital_description: The book opens rocket propellant chemistry through the specific problem Morgan solved — hydyne, the fuel that finally gave the rocket enough thrust — the Cold War space race that created the urgency, and the institutional sexism that kept Morgan's name out of the public record. The chemistry is accurate and the history is genuinely surprising.
hook: Mary Sherman Morgan invented the fuel that launched America's first satellite — and no one outside the program knew for 50 years.

title: Blizzard of Glass: The Halifax Explosion of 1917
author: Sally M. Walker
orbital_description: Opens the chemistry of the explosion (picric acid and gun cotton ignition sequence), the physics of the blast wave and its damage radius, the history of WWI munitions shipping through Halifax Harbor, the geography of Nova Scotia, and the disaster relief response including American Red Cross teams that arrived within hours from Boston.
hook: On December 6, 1917, two ships collided in Halifax Harbor — and the resulting explosion was the largest human-made blast before the atomic bomb.

title: Blockhead: The Life of Fibonacci
author: Joseph D'Agnese
orbital_description: Biography, medieval history, number theory, and natural science fused into one picture book.
hook: A medieval Italian kid everyone called Blockhead discovered the number pattern that holds sunflowers and galaxies together.

title: Blockhead: The Life of Fibonacci
author: Joseph D'Agnese
orbital_description: Leonardo Fibonacci wandered medieval Pisa seeing patterns nobody else noticed. Joseph D'Agnese tells the biography as a vindication story: the dreamy kid dismissed as a blockhead who discovered the sequence that runs through sunflowers, pinecones, seashells, and galaxies. John O'Brien's pen-and-ink illustrations layer 12th-century Italian architecture and trade routes alongside the mathematical patterns. The child absorbs biography, medieval history, number theory, and botanical geometry in a single narrative arc.
hook: The boy they called Blockhead saw numbers in everything. He was right.

title: Blueberries for Sal
author: Robert McCloskey
orbital_description: The book teaches foraging and wild food ecosystems gently; the parallel structure builds narrative comprehension; Maine's blueberry landscape is a real ecosystem children can later explore.
hook: A little girl and a bear cub both follow the wrong mother in the blueberry patch — a perfect parallel story about summer food and staying close.

title: Bomb: The Race to Build — and Steal — the World's Most Dangerous Weapon
author: Steve Sheinkin
heads_up: Contains descriptions of nuclear destruction.
orbital_description: Newbery Honor, Sibert Medal, National Book Award finalist 2012. Opens nuclear physics through the actual mechanism of fission and critical mass, the history of WWII through the Manhattan Project's geography from Oak Ridge to Los Alamos, and the Cold War's origins through the Soviet espionage network. Reads exactly like a thriller. Written with primary sources and declassified FBI interviews.
hook: Three simultaneous stories: Americans racing to build a nuclear weapon, Soviet spies racing to steal it, and Norwegian commandos racing to destroy the German program.

title: Book Scavengers
author: Jennifer Chambliss Bertman
orbital_description: Opens the biography and literary legacy of Edgar Allan Poe through a plot built around one of his mystery stories, the history of San Francisco, cryptography through cipher-based clues, and the concept of a city as a reading environment where books become coordinates.
hook: A book-based scavenger hunt across a city — and the clue hidden inside a rare book leads straight into a murder mystery.

title: Born to Fly: The First Women's Air Race Across America
author: Steve Sheinkin
orbital_description: ALSC Notable. Opens the physics of early aviation through the specific aircraft these pilots flew, the history of the women's aviation movement through the biographies of Amelia Earhart, Louise Thaden, and their competitors, the geography of the transcontinental route, and the social history of what women had to overcome to be taken seriously in a cockpit.
hook: In 1929, 20 women flew small planes across America in a race men said they couldn't handle — and only 15 landed at the finish.

title: Botticelli's Apprentice
author: Ursula Murray Husted
orbital_description: The book opens Renaissance Florence geography and social history, Botticelli's actual techniques and aesthetic philosophy, the systematic exclusion of women from formal art education in the 15th century, and the specific courage required to claim a creative identity in an institution that was not built to include you.
hook: A girl in Renaissance Florence studies with Botticelli — and the ALSC calls this colorful graphic novel inspiring and well-researched.

title: Bounce! The Wonderful, Wacky, World-Changing History of Rubber
author: Sarah Albee
orbital_description: The book opens polymer chemistry through the physical properties of natural rubber, the history of rubber from Mesoamerican ceremonial use through industrial revolution, and the chemistry of vulcanization that made rubber usable at temperature extremes. Every rubber object in modern life traces back to these discoveries.
hook: Rubber changed the modern world — and its story involves Mesoamerican ball games, Charles Goodyear's obsession, and the chemistry of vulcanization.

title: Boxes for Katje
author: Candace Fleming
heads_up: References wartime destruction.
orbital_description: Based on the real postwar care package program. Generosity cascades into an entire town being rebuilt.
hook: After WWII destroyed her Dutch town, a girl receives a box from a stranger in America, and one box becomes hundreds.

title: Boys in the Boat: The True Story of an American Team's Epic Journey to Win Gold at the 1936 Olympics (Young Adult Adaptation)
author: Greg Zeigler and Daniel James Brown
orbital_description: Young readers adaptation. Opens the physics of rowing through the concept of synchrony and swing — the specific moment when all oars move as one and the boat seems to lift — the history of the 1936 Berlin Olympics as a site of Nazi propaganda, the geography of Germany through the Olympic setting, and the social history of the Great Depression through the working-class backgrounds of the Washington rowers.
hook: Nine young men from the University of Washington, mostly from poor backgrounds, beat every Ivy League school to race against Hitler's Germany at the 1936 Berlin Olympics.

title: Brave
author: Weshoyot Alvitre
orbital_description: NPR Best 2025 opens the concept of bravery as defined by indigenous cultures — which includes cultural preservation, language maintenance, and land relationship — rather than only martial courage. Alvitre's Tongva heritage shapes an illustration style that is deeply rooted in the visual traditions of the California coast.
hook: A reclamation of what bravery means through a Native American lens — because the brave things the history books don't mention are the ones that kept a culture alive.

title: Brave Irene
author: William Steig
orbital_description: The book is an experiential weather lesson: blizzard, snow weight, wind chill, and navigation by landmarks wrapped in a determination story. Irene's problem-solving when the dress flies away is quick and resourceful.
hook: Irene delivers a ballgown through a blizzard for her sick mother — and the snow is so real you feel it.

title: Brazen: Rebel Ladies Who Rocked the World
author: Penelope Bagieu
orbital_description: Opens global history through 29 women spanning ancient Persia to 20th-century America, the graphic narrative technique as a biographical tool, the comparative history of women's constraints across different cultures and eras, and the concept of refusal as a form of historical agency. Adapted from the French bestseller.
hook: 29 women from across history and the world who refused to be what their world told them to be — each in her own chapter, drawn with precision and fury.

title: Broken
author: X. Fang
orbital_description: NYT / NYPL Best Illustrated Children's Book 2025. X. Fang's full-color illustrations draw from Chinese folk art visual traditions while telling a universal story of a child who blames an innocent party rather than confessing. The resolution is earned rather than lectured.
hook: A child breaks grandmother's favorite cup — and blames the cat. The moral arc is simple; the illustration is astonishing.

title: Brown Girl Dreaming
author: Jacqueline Woodson
heads_up: Racial discrimination, Jim Crow, family complexity.
orbital_description: Memoir in verse. The Great Migration, Jim Crow, and the birth of a writer. National Book Award, Newbery Honor.
hook: Jacqueline Woodson grew up between Ohio and South Carolina with the Civil Rights Movement happening outside her window.

title: Brown Girl Dreaming
author: Jacqueline Woodson
heads_up: Depicts segregation, racial violence (referenced, not graphic), family separation, and religious tension. The verse form creates emotional distance that makes it accessible for 10+.
orbital_description: Woodson's verse memoir covers her childhood from the 1960s through the early 1970s: the grandparents in Greenville, the mother in Brooklyn, the father who stayed behind, the siblings who held the family together. Each poem orbits a different intersection: Jim Crow geography and northern migration, Jehovah's Witness faith and secular schooling, oral storytelling tradition and written literacy, and the moment a child discovers that words are the material she was made to work with. The verse form is the content: Woodson proves that poetry can hold history, family, and geography simultaneously.
hook: Jacqueline Woodson grew up between South Carolina and New York, between the civil rights movement and her own imagination. She wrote it all down.

title: Building Our House
author: Jonathan Bean
orbital_description: Jonathan Bean drew this from his own childhood: his family bought land and built a house themselves over the course of a year. The illustrations carry construction sequencing with engineering-textbook precision: foundation pouring, framing, plumbing, wiring, roofing, finishing. Each season brings a new phase. The child absorbs structural engineering, tool identification, seasonal construction constraints, plumbing and electrical systems, and the economics of sweat equity. This is not a metaphor for building. It is actual building.
hook: A family builds their own house from the ground up. Every board, every nail, every season.

title: Busted
author: Dan Gemeinhart
orbital_description: The ALSC 2026 Notable Book opens the mechanics of wrongful accusation, the social psychology of assumed guilt, and the courage required to pursue truth in a community that has decided. Gemeinhart consistently gives MG readers moral complexity without flinching from the actual difficulty of doing the right thing.
hook: A boy is wrongly accused — and the whole town has already made up its mind.

title: By the Great Horn Spoon!
author: Sid Fleischman
orbital_description: The book opens Gold Rush history, the real Cape Horn sailing route, the economics of the 1849 California rush, and the social class dynamics of a proper English butler in absolute chaos. Every scheme Praiseworthy runs is period-accurate.
hook: A Boston boy and his aunt's butler sail around Cape Horn to the California Gold Rush — and the butler's dignity is his greatest survival tool.

title: Caboose
author: Travis Jonker
orbital_description: Kirkus Spring 2025 Preview. Travis Jonker and Ruth Chan open the social dynamics of classroom hierarchy through the specific experience of a child who has built their identity around being first, the concept that being last can teach something being first never did, and the social-emotional mechanics of how communities handle fairness.
hook: A hippo is always first in line — until the day she's banished to the back. What happens to a child who has always led?

title: Call Me Roberto!: The Story of Roberto Clemente
author: Nathalie Alonso
orbital_description: The 2024 Sibert Honor book opens Puerto Rican baseball history, the economics and geography of Carolina, Puerto Rico, the specific racism Clemente faced as a Black Latino player in 1950s-1960s America, and the humanitarian work that defined the last years of his life.
hook: Roberto Clemente refused to be called Bob — and that refusal was the beginning of everything else he refused.

title: Caps for Sale
author: Esphyr Slobodkina
orbital_description: Stacking order, color patterns, counting, primate mimicry behavior, and street vendor economics.
hook: A peddler falls asleep under a tree with caps stacked on his head. Every monkey in the tree takes one.

title: Castle
author: David Macaulay
heads_up: Military context. No graphic violence.
orbital_description: Pen-and-ink drawings are architectural blueprints rendered as art. Every engineering decision explained.
hook: Every stone in a medieval castle was cut, carried, lifted, and placed by hand. Caldecott Honor.

title: Cece Loves Science
author: Kimberly Derting
orbital_description: Kimberly Derting walks Cece through the full scientific method with zero shortcuts: observation, question, hypothesis, experiment design, data collection, analysis, conclusion. The question is simple (does her dog have a favorite color?) but the process is rigorous. Shelli R. Johannes' illustrations carry the data tables, bar charts, and experimental controls while the text carries the vocabulary. The child absorbs the scientific method as a repeatable practice, not a worksheet concept. Cece's conclusion is unexpected, which is the whole point.
hook: Cece has a question about her dog. She does not Google it. She designs an experiment.

title: Change Is in the Air: The Hidden Discoveries of Eunice Newton Foote, the First Climate Scientist
author: Rebecca Donnelly
orbital_description: Three starred reviews (Kirkus, PW, Booklist) for the picture book biography of the first woman to identify the greenhouse effect. Opens climate chemistry through Foote's CO2 experiments, the history of the Seneca Falls women's rights convention she signed, the science of atmospheric warming, and the specific mechanisms by which women's scientific work was erased and reassigned in the 19th century.
hook: Eunice Newton Foote discovered that CO2 traps heat in the atmosphere in 1856 — and because she was a woman, John Tyndall got the credit three years later.

title: Charlotte's Web
author: E.B. White
orbital_description: Charlotte's Web opens spider biology including web construction and egg sacs, farm ecology, seasonal cycles, and the mechanics of persuasion through written language. The death of Charlotte is the finest introduction to mortality in children's literature.
hook: A spider writes words in her web to save a pig — and asks children to think about friendship, mortality, and what a life well spent looks like.

title: Chasing Lincoln's Killer
author: James L. Swanson
orbital_description: Adapted from the adult bestseller Manhunt. Opens the forensics of the assassination investigation through specific evidence and witnesses, the geography of Booth's escape route through Maryland and Virginia, the history of the immediate post-Civil-War period, and the concept of a manhunt as a law enforcement operation with specific tactical constraints.
hook: John Wilkes Booth shot Abraham Lincoln at 10:15 PM on April 14, 1865 — and for 12 days evaded the largest manhunt in American history, with a broken leg.

title: Chasing Space: An Astronaut's Story of Grit, Grace, and Second Chances
author: Leland Melvin
orbital_description: Opens the physics of space travel and microgravity through Melvin's first-hand experience aboard the shuttle, the biology of the inner ear and deafness from hyperbaric exposure during underwater training, the history of NASA's astronaut selection, and the concept of a second chance as a structural feature of ambitious lives.
hook: Leland Melvin was drafted by the NFL and went deaf in NASA training — and still made it to the International Space Station twice.

title: Chasing Vermeer
author: Blue Balliett
orbital_description: The book opens Vermeer's actual painting A Lady Writing, pentomino mathematics, and the logic of pattern recognition. The Chicago Hyde Park neighborhood is real geography. The solution requires the same logic the characters use.
hook: Two Chicago sixth-graders investigate the theft of a Vermeer painting using a code hidden in pentomino puzzles.

title: Chicka Chicka Boom Boom
author: Bill Martin Jr.
orbital_description: The cumulative rhyme builds phonological awareness and letter recognition simultaneously. The uppercase letters rescuing lowercase letters is a neat structural lesson in letter forms children absorb without noticing.
hook: All 26 letters race up the coconut tree — a phonics party with a structural collapse and a rhythm that sticks for life.

title: Chooch Helped
author: Andrea L. Rogers
orbital_description: The 2025 Caldecott Medal winner captures the specific exhaustion of being the older sibling when the younger one is always just 'helping.' The book opens sibling dynamics, developmental stages, and family negotiation through Kunz's vibrant illustrations with deep emotional accuracy.
hook: Sissy's little brother Chooch helps with everything — whether anyone asked him to or not — and the frustration is exactly right.

title: Chrysanthemum
author: Kevin Henkes
orbital_description: The book opens linguistics including names, etymology, and the weight of words, alongside the social dynamics of belonging and teasing, and the extraordinary power a teacher has to reshape a child's sense of self.
hook: A girl named Chrysanthemum loves her name until school teaches her to be ashamed of it — and one teacher quietly gives it back.

title: City of Plagues and Perfumes
author: Jake Halpern and Peter Kujawinski
orbital_description: PW Spring 2026 Sneak Preview. From the co-authors of the Pillage trilogy. Opens the history of epidemics and how disease spreads, the science of olfaction and the chemistry of scent, and the adventure structure of a child using an unusual biological gift as a scientific instrument. Unique premise with genuine science underneath.
hook: A girl with a remarkable sense of smell discovers she can track a plague through its scent — and must use her ability to save a city.

title: City Safari: A Young Naturalist's Guide to Discovering Nature in the City
author: Jenette Restivo
orbital_description: Fall 2025 pub. Opens urban ecology through the specific habitats found in cities — storm drains, rooftops, vacant lots, street trees — and the organisms that colonize each one. Models citizen science methodology through field observation protocols, habitat mapping, and species identification techniques applicable immediately after reading.
hook: The city is a wildlife refuge hiding in plain sight — and this field guide gives children the tools to find it, identify it, and record it.

title: City Spies
author: James Ponti
orbital_description: Opens international geography through missions across multiple countries, the history of British intelligence and the real structure of MI6, tradecraft (surveillance, dead drops, cover identities) as real skills, and the found-family dynamic of people who have no one else. Clean, fast, and genuinely fun.
hook: Five kids from five different countries, each with a skill set no adult spy could fake — and MI6 recruits them to go where trained agents cannot.

title: City Summer, Country Summer
author: Kiese Laymon
orbital_description: Confirmed April 1, 2025. Kiese Laymon (Heavy) opens the social-emotional architecture of Black childhood joy across two distinct geographies — the specific sensory world of a Southern family visit and the urban summer — and the concept of belonging as something that can exist in multiple places at once.
hook: Three Black boys spend a summer in the city and a summer in the country, finding that both are home in ways neither could have expected.

title: Clementine
author: Sara Pennypacker
orbital_description: The book opens Boston neighborhood geography, the social dynamics of third grade, and the specific texture of a highly creative and impulsive child's mind. Clementine's logic is always internally consistent; the problems are always spectacular.
hook: Clementine has a terrible week — but the disasters are all her own very logical ideas, and her relationship with her principal is the funniest in chapter book literature.

title: Click Clack Moo: Cows That Type
author: Doreen Cronin
orbital_description: Beneath the humor is real civics: collective bargaining, written demands, third-party mediation, and the power of literacy. Duck's role as mediator introduces negotiation structure. Children absorb how organizations communicate formally before they know what formal means.
hook: The cows find a typewriter and write demands to the farmer — a barnyard negotiation that accidentally teaches labor history and the power of written language.

title: Clifford the Big Red Dog
author: Norman Bridwell
orbital_description: The book teaches consequence, scale, and community adaptation through cheerful logic: what does a dog the size of a house actually need? What changes? What stays the same? The love-causes-growth premise opens conversation about caring for animals.
hook: Emily Elizabeth's love for her tiny puppy makes him the biggest dog in the world — and the town has to rearrange itself around Clifford.

title: Code Girls: The Untold Story of the American Women Codebreakers of World War II (Young Readers Edition)
author: Liza Mundy
orbital_description: Opens cryptography and the mathematics of code-breaking through the specific methods used by these women, the history of the NSA's predecessor agencies through their WWII work, the geography of Washington DC's wartime facilities, and the concept of classified work and why these women couldn't tell anyone what they'd done even after the war ended.
hook: Thousands of American women were secretly recruited to crack enemy codes in WWII — doing mathematics that shortened the war — and the government kept it classified for decades.

title: Cora's Beach Adventure
author: Sally M. Walker
orbital_description: PW Spring 2026 Sneak Preview. Opens tidal ecology including the zones of a tide pool, the biology of organisms that survive between tides, and the scientific method of observation and question-asking through a child's beach day.
hook: A day of science-based discovery at the shore — tide pools, sand, birds, and the scientific method modeled through genuine curiosity.

title: Corduroy
author: Don Freeman
orbital_description: The book teaches acceptance of imperfection, the value of persistence, and the social geography of a department store. Corduroy's overnight adventure models courage; Lisa's return models commitment.
hook: A department store bear with a missing button waits for someone to love him — and a girl with not quite enough money proves that love doesn't need to be perfect.

title: Count on Me
author: Miguel Tanco
orbital_description: Miguel Tanco drew a child whose favorite subject is math, and then showed what that actually looks like: not worksheets, but spirals in sunflowers, symmetry in butterfly wings, patterns in city grids. The book orbits numeracy, geometry, natural science, and art simultaneously because it treats math as a way of seeing, not a set of problems to solve. The parent-child relationship anchors the whole thing. After this one, your child will count everything.
hook: A girl who sees math everywhere: in spider webs, in sliced oranges, in the way birds sit on a wire.

title: Counting on Katherine
author: Helaine Becker
orbital_description: Picture book companion to Hidden Figures focusing on Katherine Johnson and Apollo 13.
hook: When Apollo 13 was lost in space, the person NASA called was a Black woman with a pencil.

title: Cranky, Crabby Crow (Saves the World)
author: Corey R. Tabor
orbital_description: SLJ Best Picture Book 2025. Corey R. Tabor (2024 and 2025 Geisel Award winner) opens the superhero narrative structure through the twist of a character whose antisocial behavior turns out to be purposeful, modeling the concept that appearances rarely explain motivations. The crow's actual behavior is built on corvid intelligence and community care.
hook: Crow seems cranky and mean and scares everyone away — and he is actually on a top-secret mission that will stun his friends.

title: Creep, Leap, Crunch! A Food Chain Story
author: Jody Jensen Shaffer
orbital_description: The book opens the science of food chains through the specific energy transfer from producers to consumers to decomposers, the habitats of each linked creature, and the concept that food chains are more accurately food webs. Neal's geometric illustrations make the connections visual before the text names them.
hook: From worm to robin to hawk to decomposer — the food chain is a circle, and this book walks it complete.

title: Creepy Carrots!
author: Aaron Reynolds
orbital_description: Aaron Reynolds writes a horror-comedy for toddlers: Jasper Rabbit eats carrots from Crackenhopper Field every day until the carrots appear to follow him home, lurk in his bathtub, and stare at him from the closet. Peter Brown's retro noir illustrations (black, white, and orange only) carry the atmosphere of a 1950s horror film while the plot carries the vegetable garden cycle, the psychology of guilt and imagination, and a twist ending where Jasper's solution (fencing off the field) is exactly what the carrots wanted. The child absorbs plant growth, humor as a fear-management tool, and narrative irony.
hook: Jasper Rabbit loves carrots. Then the carrots start following him. Or do they?

title: Croco
author: Azul López
orbital_description: The 2026 Batchelder Award winner opens the social-emotional concept of persistence through failure, the engineering mindset of iterative problem-solving, and the relief of finally finding what works. Translated from Spanish, López's visual humor is physical and universal — this works for very young readers who can't yet read a word.
hook: Croco is stuck in a hole — and tries every solution imaginable before finding the one that actually works.

title: Dad
author: Christian Robinson
orbital_description: Kirkus starred ('Robinson outdoes himself') from MacKids 2026 roundup. Christian Robinson (Last Stop on Market Street, Caldecott Honor; Gaston, multiple starred reviews) opens the social-emotional complexity of father-child relationships with honesty and tenderness. The signature collage illustration style renders emotion through color and composition rather than explanation.
hook: Christian Robinson's love letter to his own father — depicting a dad who is vulnerable, imperfect, gentle, and deeply human.

title: Dad, I Miss You
author: Nadia Sammurtok
heads_up: PUB YEAR NEEDS VERIFICATION — Charlotte Huck Honor 2025 = likely 2024 pub.
orbital_description: Charlotte Huck Honor 2025. Opens the social-emotional experience of a child's longing for an absent parent through the sensory geography of an Inuit landscape, the concept of how missing someone is its own form of connection, and Inuit family and cultural life. NOTE: May be 2024 pub — verify before placing in 2025 batch.
hook: An Inuit child misses their father — and the specific landscape of the Arctic holds both the missing and the love.

title: Daughter of the Light-Footed People: The Story of Indigenous Marathon Champion Lorena Ramírez
author: Belen Medina
orbital_description: ALSC 2026 Notable Recordings (implies 2025 pub). Opens the physiology of distance running through the Tarahumara tradition of ultra-endurance running, the geography and culture of the Copper Canyon region of Chihuahua, and the specific story of Lorena Ramírez who won elite ultramarathons wearing huarache sandals and carrying a traditional basket.
hook: The Tarahumara people of northern Mexico have run ultramarathons in homemade sandals for centuries — and Lorena Ramírez became an international champion while remaining completely true to her people's tradition.

title: Dawn: Watch the World Awaken
author: Marc Martin
orbital_description: The 2026 ALSC Notable Book opens nature observation through the specific sequence of dawn: which animals stir first and why, the physics of light changing the landscape, the ecology of a forest lake at sunrise. Martin's art makes the moment feel like witnessing something private and true.
hook: Dawn breaks over a forest lake — and every creature's awakening is a precise observation of how nature opens each day.

title: Dead Ends! Flukes, Flops, and Failures That Sparked Medical Marvels
author: Lindsey Fitzharris
heads_up: Contains historical medical procedures and descriptions of disease.
orbital_description: SLJ 2025 Stars So Far starred review. From the author of Plague-Busters! and The Butchering Art, Fitzharris opens medical history through the specific failures that accidentally led to penicillin, anesthesia, X-rays, and other breakthroughs, the concept of productive failure as a scientific methodology, and the history of medicine as a long series of wrong turns that eventually found the right direction.
hook: Every major medical breakthrough was preceded by a spectacular failure — and the failures are often more interesting than the successes.

title: Debbie Allen's Rise to Fame
author: Tami Charles
orbital_description: Fall 2025 pub. Tami Charles opens the history of racial segregation in arts education through Debbie Allen's specific experience being rejected from the Houston Foundation for Ballet because of her race, the subsequent path through Broadway and Hollywood to Fame and Grey's Anatomy, and the concept of creative work as a form of sustained resistance.
hook: Debbie Allen fought racism to study ballet as a child — and became one of the most powerful figures in American television and dance.

title: Diary of a Spider
author: Doreen Cronin
orbital_description: The book introduces arachnid biology including webs, molting, and silk production through the same comic diary format as its companion, while exploring cross-species relationships and the experience of being feared.
hook: A spider's diary reveals the misunderstood life of arachnids — including web architecture, fly diplomacy, and the annual spider molt.

title: Diary of a Worm
author: Doreen Cronin
orbital_description: The book opens decomposition, soil health, underground ecosystems, and the role of earthworms in composting, all through a first-person diary format that models narrative perspective. After this one, children look at dirt differently.
hook: A worm's diary reveals the underground world most children have never thought about — and makes soil ecology genuinely unforgettable.

title: Digging Up Dinosaurs
author: Aliki
orbital_description: Answers the question kids never think to ask: how did the skeleton get into the museum?
hook: The dinosaur in the museum did not walk in through the front door. Someone dug it up bone by bone.

title: Discovering Life's Story: The Birth of Genetics
author: Joy Hakim
orbital_description: PW Spring 2026 Sneak Preview. Continuation of Joy Hakim's acclaimed series charting human scientific discovery (A History of US). Opens genetics through the complete arc from Mendel's monastery garden experiments through Rosalind Franklin, Watson and Crick, and the Human Genome Project, with the history of scientific credit and erasure woven throughout.
hook: The story of how humans figured out that traits are inherited, from Gregor Mendel's peas to the discovery of DNA — and how long it took, and how many people were wrong first.

title: Doctor De Soto
author: William Steig
orbital_description: The book is a clean ethics and strategy puzzle: the fox breaks trust; the De Sotos use cleverness to protect themselves without abandoning professional duty. The glue solution is elegant and funny.
hook: A mouse dentist treats a fox — and has to outwit the fox before the fox eats him at the end of the appointment.

title: Don't Eat the Birthday Boy!
author: Donna Barba Higuera
orbital_description: Kirkus starred, from the MacKids 2026 roundup. Donna Barba Higuera (The Last Cuentista, Pura Belpré winner) opens the comic conflict between a monster's assignment and a child's birthday party, modeling the social-emotional concept of resisting impulse in favor of relationship. The humor is sharp and the emotional resolution is earned.
hook: A monster was assigned to scare a child — but the child is having a birthday party, and the monster has a problem.

title: Don't Let the Pigeon Drive the Bus
author: Mo Willems
orbital_description: The book breaks the fourth wall to make children active participants; the pigeon's escalating negotiation tactics introduce persuasion, argumentation, and the art of saying no. Every tactic the pigeon uses is a real rhetorical strategy.
hook: The bus driver asks you to keep the pigeon from driving — and the pigeon will try every rhetorical trick he knows.

title: Don't Trust Fish
author: Neil Sharpson
orbital_description: NPR named this one of the funniest picture books of 2025. Sharpson's conspiracy-theory premise and Santat's cinematic illustrations build a joke that pays off on every spread. Beneath the absurdity is a genuine lesson in unreliable narration and the difference between assertion and evidence.
hook: Fish have been plotting against us the whole time — and this book is a warning delivered with the comedic timing of a master.

title: Dragon Flower
author: Chen Jiang Hong
orbital_description: The NYT Best Illustrated 2025 winner opens Chinese mythology through the dragon as a water and weather deity, the history of Chinese ink brush painting as a traditional art form, the geography of classical Chinese landscapes, and the ecological concept of interdependence. Translated from the French by Alyson Waters.
hook: A mythological tale from China about a dragon and a flower whose fates are bound together — illustrated with brush painting techniques that are works of art.

title: Dragons Love Tacos
author: Adam Rubin
orbital_description: The book is pure delight engineering: the cause-and-effect logic of spicy salsa causing dragon fire is genuinely funny and genuinely logical. It rewards careful attention and predicting what happens next.
hook: Dragons love tacos — but they have a fatal weakness for salsa that triggers the funniest consequence in picture book history.

title: Drawn Onward
author: Daniel Nayeri
heads_up: Depicts the death of a loved one; discuss before reading if needed.
orbital_description: The book opens the emotional structure of grief through a formal literary device: every line of text is a palindrome, meaning the book reads identically from both directions. The wordplay introduces linguistic pattern recognition while the emotional content models loss with extraordinary gentleness.
hook: The text reads the same forward and backward — and so does grief, which takes you somewhere new while feeling like you're going in reverse.

title: Drowned City: Hurricane Katrina and New Orleans
author: Don Brown
heads_up: Contains depictions of death and disaster.
orbital_description: Sibert Honor 2016. Opens the engineering of levee systems through what failed and why (soil composition, inadequate height, design flaws), the physics of storm surge and flooding, the geography of New Orleans as a below-sea-level city surrounded by water, and the social history of which neighborhoods were flooded vs. protected and why those patterns followed racial and economic lines.
hook: Hurricane Katrina hit New Orleans on August 29, 2005 — and the real disaster was not the storm, but the levees that were supposed to protect the city.

title: Drum Dream Girl
author: Margarita Engle
orbital_description: Based on Millo Castro Zaldarriaga, who broke Cuba's taboo against female drummers. Engle's verse carries rhythm in its line breaks, so the child hears the drumming before understanding the history. Rafael Lopez's illustrations layer Havana's architecture, tropical ecology, and Afro-Cuban musical tradition into every page. The story orbits music theory, Caribbean geography, gender history, and the physics of percussion without teaching any of them directly.
hook: On an island where only boys played drums, one girl heard music anyway.

title: Duck and Goose
author: Tad Hills
orbital_description: The book models conflict over a shared resource, the humiliation of being wrong, and the repair of a friendship built on a mistake. The polka-dotted ball is a gentle lesson in assumptions.
hook: Duck and Goose fight over a polka-dotted ball that turns out to be an egg — and figure out how to be friends despite the misunderstanding.

title: Each Kindness
author: Jacqueline Woodson
heads_up: The ending is unresolved. The missed chance stays missed. This is intentional and may upset younger readers who expect resolution.
orbital_description: Jacqueline Woodson does something almost no children's book does: she does not resolve the cruelty. Chloe excludes the new girl, Maya. The teacher does a lesson on kindness as ripples in a pond. Maya moves away. Chloe realizes too late. The book ends with Chloe standing at the pond, understanding that some kindnesses, once missed, stay missed. E.B. Lewis's watercolors carry the weight of winter light and empty chairs. The child absorbs social dynamics, the ripple-effect metaphor (physics of wave propagation), and the permanent cost of inaction.
hook: Maya was new. Chloe was not kind to her. Then Maya moved away, and the chance was gone.

title: Eagle Drums
author: Nasugraq Rainey Hopson
orbital_description: The 2024 Newbery Honor winner opens Inuit cultural traditions including drum dancing, shamanic practice, and oral history, alongside Alaskan coastal ecology and the specific geography of the North Slope. Hopson drew on her own Inupiaq heritage to create the first Newbery Honor book authored and illustrated by the same Indigenous artist.
hook: A boy must master the drum dance of his Inuit ancestors to save his village — and the drums teach him something about power that his grandfather never could.

title: Earthrise: How the Apollo 8 Crew Photographed the World
author: Leonard S. Marcus
orbital_description: Multiple starred-review nonfiction book opens the physics of space photography, the history of the Apollo 8 mission as the first to orbit the moon, the geopolitical context of 1968 and the Cold War, and the environmental and philosophical impact of the Earthrise photograph on the conservation movement. Marcus argues the photograph is one of the most consequential images ever taken.
hook: The photograph taken by Apollo 8 astronauts in December 1968 showed Earth as a small blue marble floating in space — and it changed how humans saw themselves and their planet.

title: El Deafo
author: Cece Bell
orbital_description: Newbery Honor 2015. Opens the biology of hearing including how the cochlea processes sound and what meningitis damages, the history of hearing aid technology through the specific Phonic Ear Bell wears, and the social-emotional experience of disability and identity through the graphic memoir form's ability to show interior experience.
hook: Cece Bell lost her hearing at 4 from meningitis — and got the biggest hearing aid any child had ever worn, which she eventually realized gave her a superpower.

title: Elatsoe
author: Darcie Little Badger
orbital_description: American Indian Youth Literature Award. Opens Lipan Apache oral traditions and cultural practices, the concept of indigenous land sovereignty in Texas, the mystery genre structure elevated by a protagonist whose power is genuinely grounded in cultural heritage, and the physics of what a ghost actually is in the Lipan Apache framework.
hook: Elatsoe can wake the ghosts of dead animals — and when her cousin is murdered in a perfect town that forbids murder, she uses that gift to find out why.

title: Elf Dog and Owl Head
author: M.T. Anderson
orbital_description: The 2024 Newbery Honor novel opens suburban ecology and geography through a magical realist lens, the philosophy of transformation, and the specific anxiety of not knowing whether the strange thing you love is safe. Anderson's dark comic sensibility and Wu's ethereal illustrations create something genuinely uncategorizable.
hook: A boy adopts a strange dog from the suburbs and discovers that some friendships require you to become a different version of yourself.

title: Enemy Pie
author: Derek Munson
orbital_description: The book is a clean lesson in assumption, prejudice, and the mechanics of friendship: proximity, shared experience, and play dissolve hostility faster than any lecture. The father's method models conflict-resolution wisdom children can actually use.
hook: A boy spends a whole summer day with his enemy to make the pie work — and discovers his enemy isn't an enemy at all.

title: Escape from Mr. Lemoncello's Library
author: Chris Grabenstein
orbital_description: Opens the history of libraries through a puzzle-game built around literary knowledge, information literacy (finding what you need in a vast collection), the Dewey Decimal System as an actual navigational tool, and the dynamics of collaboration vs. competition under pressure. The puzzles are real and solvable.
hook: A famous game designer built the most extraordinary library ever — and 12 kids are locked inside for the night to solve puzzles using only the books on the shelves.

title: Every Monday Mabel
author: Jashar Awan
orbital_description: The 2026 Caldecott Honor Book with five starred reviews opens the social-emotional concept of passion without apology: Mabel loves what she loves and doesn't require external validation. The community helpers angle introduces the real work that keeps neighborhoods functioning. The bold, primary-color illustration style is immediately arresting.
hook: Every Monday, Mabel drags her chair to the driveway to watch the garbage truck — and she doesn't care even slightly that her family thinks it's weird.

title: Everywhere, Wonder
author: Matthew Swanson
orbital_description: Matthew Swanson and Robbi Behr built this book around questions, not answers. Why do leaves change color? Where does the wind start? The illustrations move through biomes and seasons, carrying ecology, meteorology, and botany in the background while the text models scientific curiosity as a daily practice. The child finishes this book wanting to go outside and look at something closely, which is the entire point.
hook: A book that teaches your child to ask questions the way a scientist does: by looking harder at ordinary things.

title: Extra Yarn
author: Mac Barnett
orbital_description: The book opens color theory, textile science, community transformation, and the economics of abundance. The archduke's obsession with the magical yarn is a lesson in greed; Annabelle's generosity is its own kind of physics.
hook: Annabelle finds a box of yarn that never runs out — and knits color into a gray winter town until everyone has more than they need.

title: Extreme Birdwatching: Measuring Change on a Galápagos Island
author: Loree Griffin Burns
orbital_description: Lit Hub Most Anticipated 2026. Part of the Discovery Chronicles chapter book series from MIT Kids Press. Opens evolutionary biology through the Grant fieldwork that produced direct observational evidence of natural selection happening in real time, the geography of the Galápagos archipelago, the scientific methodology of longitudinal field research, and the history of Darwin's own finch observations as the starting point. Pub March 3, 2026.
hook: Rosemary and Peter Grant spent 40 years on a remote Galápagos island watching finches — and proved that evolution happens far faster than Darwin ever imagined.

title: Fablehaven
author: Brandon Mull
orbital_description: The book is built on ecological logic: the preserve is a managed habitat for creatures that would destroy each other and humans without governance. The creatures follow internally consistent rules. Connecticut geography anchors the fantasy.
hook: A brother and sister discover their grandparents' Connecticut farm is a secret preserve for magical creatures — and someone is trying to break it open.

title: Fall Is for Beginnings
author: Rajani LaRocca
orbital_description: Confirmed August 5, 2025. Rajani LaRocca opens the ecology of autumn through the phenological signs of the season's arrival, the social-emotional experience of beginning a new school year in an unfamiliar place, and the concept that every new beginning is also the start of something you haven't named yet.
hook: The start of the school year is also the start of fall — and Ravi discovers that both arrivals bring more than he expected.

title: Fallout: Spies, Superbombs, and the Ultimate Cold War Showdown
author: Steve Sheinkin
orbital_description: Sequel to Bomb. Opens the physics of thermonuclear fusion vs. fission, the history of the Cold War through the arms race and the Cuban Missile Crisis, the careers of the spies who transferred nuclear secrets, and the moral biography of Oppenheimer who built the first bomb and then opposed the second.
hook: The hydrogen bomb was 1,000 times more powerful than the atom bomb — and as America built it, Soviet spies stole the secrets, and the world reached the edge of annihilation.

title: Fia Hosein Finds Her Beat
author: Tracey Baptiste
orbital_description: Lit Hub Most Anticipated 2026, praised by Kate Messner. Tracey Baptiste (The Jumbies, African Icons) opens the linguistics of accent and language shift in immigrant children, the geography and culture of Trinidad, and the specific social-emotional experience of assimilation as loss rather than gain. Part of The Kids in Mrs. Z's Class series — readable in any order.
hook: Fia just immigrated from Trinidad to Peppermint Falls — and she's terrified that as her accent fades, she's losing the part of herself that is most her.

title: Fighting With Love: The Legacy of John Lewis
author: Lesa Cline-Ransome
heads_up: Depicts racial violence; discuss before reading.
orbital_description: ALSC 2026 Notable Recordings list (implies 2025 pub). Lesa Cline-Ransome opens the civil rights movement through John Lewis's specific biography including the Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee, the Freedom Riders, Bloody Sunday on the Edmund Pettus Bridge, and his 33 years in Congress. The concept of 'good trouble' is the throughline.
hook: John Lewis was beaten on the Edmund Pettus Bridge and went back 54 more times to walk it until the law finally changed — and this is the story of what he called 'good trouble.'

title: Finding Home: Amazing Places Animals Live
author: Mike Unwin
orbital_description: Confirmed March 4, 2025. Jenni Desmond (The Polar Bear, ALSC Notable) opens habitat ecology through the science of how specific animal adaptations map precisely to habitat conditions, the geography of twelve distinct ecosystems worldwide, and the concept of ecological niche as the space where an animal's biology meets its environment.
hook: Twelve animals, twelve extraordinary habitats — from the deep ocean to Arctic ice to city storm drains — each adapted in ways that make survival look almost impossible and completely elegant.

title: Fireworks
author: Matthew Burgess
orbital_description: The 2026 Caldecott Medal winner opens light physics through the fireworks display, urban geography through a city neighborhood in summer heat, and the science of anticipation. Chien's vertical gatefold pages open like a real explosion of color.
hook: Two siblings count down a steamy July day to the fireworks — and the waiting is almost the whole point.

title: Fish and Wave
author: Sergio Ruzzier
orbital_description: The 2023 Geisel Honor book opens ocean science through the contrasting characters of a creature that lives in water and water itself. The humor is dry and the logic is consistent: what does it mean to be friends with something that is always moving? The early reader format builds reading stamina through short high-interest chapters.
hook: A fish and a wave are total opposites — and they become best friends anyway, which is the whole ocean in a sentence.

title: Flat Stanley
author: Jeff Brown
orbital_description: The book introduces US geography through physical comedy. Stanley's problem-solving with his flatness is pure lateral thinking. The mailing sequence maps postal geography and opens conversation about distance and transportation.
hook: Stanley Lambchop wakes up flat and discovers that being flat has unexpected advantages — including being mailed to California.

title: Flower Talk
author: Sara Levine
orbital_description: Sara Levine reveals that flower colors, patterns, shapes, and scents are communication systems evolved to attract specific pollinators. UV nectar guides visible only to bees. Red petals that hummingbirds see but bees cannot. Night-blooming flowers that signal moths through scent. Masha D'yans' watercolors carry botanical precision while the text carries pollination ecology, co-evolution, and the idea that what humans experience as beauty is actually a conversation between species. The child absorbs plant anatomy, pollinator biology, electromagnetic spectrum basics, and evolutionary strategy.
hook: Flowers are not just pretty. They are talking. Every petal, every scent, every stripe is a message to a pollinator.

title: Footeprint: Eunice Newton Foote at the Dawn of Climate Science and Women's Rights
author: Lindsay H. Metcalf
orbital_description: Pub Feb 10, 2026. Kirkus reviewed. Lindsay Metcalf (Beatrix Potter Scientist, Farmers Unite!) opens the chemistry of greenhouse gases through Foote's 1856 CO2 experiments, the history of the Seneca Falls Convention which Foote signed, the institutional machinery that credited John Tyndall with her discovery, and the patent system that Foote used to claim credit for her inventions when science conferences wouldn't let her speak.
hook: Eunice Newton Foote discovered the greenhouse effect in 1856 and was erased from history so completely that it took 150 years for anyone to notice — this is the novel that refuses to let that stand.

title: Found Sound
author: Meg Wolitzer and Charlie Panek
orbital_description: PW Spring 2026 preview. Co-written by Meg Wolitzer (one of America's most acclaimed adult novelists, making her children's debut) and her son. Opens acoustics and the physics of sound through the scavenger hunt structure, the concept of found sound as a musical tradition, and the community history embedded in music.
hook: Felix and his neighbor Marigold follow a series of musical clues on a sound-based scavenger hunt through their town — and the music they find opens the history of their community.

title: Fox Has a Problem
author: Corey R. Tabor
orbital_description: The 2024 Geisel Award winner opens the logic of problem definition and the comedy of mismatched solutions: when everyone solves the wrong problem, the original problem escalates. The early reader format makes the logic ladder easy to follow. The animals' solutions are internally consistent, which makes the humor land.
hook: Fox has a problem — and every animal friend he asks gives him a completely different solution, which makes the problem much, much bigger.

title: Fox Versus Fox
author: Corey R. Tabor
orbital_description: The 2025 Geisel Award winner opens identity formation through the comic logic of two identical characters arguing about authenticity. The early reader format uses the competition structure to build comprehension of comparison while delivering a sharp lesson about the cost of constant rivalry.
hook: Fox meets another Fox — and they are immediately competitive about everything, including who is more Fox-like.

title: Frederick
author: Leo Lionni
orbital_description: Frederick opens art, seasonal ecology, community roles, and the invisible labor of creative work. The book makes children ask what counts as useful — a question that leads into economics, ecology, and the human need for beauty.
hook: While his mouse family stores food for winter, Frederick stores colors, words, and sunlight — and saves them all when the cold and dark close in.

title: Freight Train
author: Donald Crews
orbital_description: Color identification, sequencing, transportation infrastructure, and physics of motion.
