hook: A red caboose, an orange tank car, a yellow hopper car, and a black steam engine. Moving. Caldecott Honor.

title: Frizzy
author: Claribel A. Ortega
orbital_description: The 2023 Belpré Award winner opens Dominican-American cultural identity, the history of European beauty standards imposed on Black and Latina women, family intergenerational patterns, and the social economics of hair in a community where straightening is a ritual. The graphic novel format makes the visual transformation deeply felt.
hook: Marlene wants to straighten her curly hair to fit in — and the journey back to her natural hair is the journey back to herself.

title: Frog and Toad Are Friends
author: Arnold Lobel
orbital_description: Frog and Toad model different personality types, problem-solving styles, and seasonal rhythms. The stories teach empathy through contrast: Frog is proactive, Toad is anxious, and their friendship works anyway.
hook: Five short stories about two very different friends — and the most loving argument for loyalty in early chapter book form.

title: From Seed to Plant
author: Gail Gibbons
orbital_description: The book covers pollination with named insect pollinators, seed dispersal mechanisms, germination, and plant anatomy with labeled diagrams. It is the foundation for every plant science conversation for years to come.
hook: The complete plant life cycle from seed to flower to fruit to new seed — accurate, illustrated, and impossible to forget.

title: From the Mixed-Up Files of Mrs. Basil E. Frankweiler
author: E.L. Konigsburg
orbital_description: Newbery Medal 1968. Opens the collections of the Metropolitan Museum of Art, art historical methods for authenticating Renaissance works, New York City geography, and the concept of living invisibly inside a public institution. The survival logistics make it an engineering puzzle.
hook: Two siblings secretly live inside the Metropolitan Museum of Art in New York City — and stumble onto a mystery about a statue that may be by Michelangelo.

title: Full Moon
author: Michael Light
orbital_description: Michael Light curated 129 photographs from NASA's master negatives, digitally remastered from the original Apollo mission film. The sequence follows a complete lunar journey: Earth departure, transit, lunar orbit, descent, surface walk, ascent, return. No text beyond brief captions. The child absorbs lunar geology, spacecraft engineering, the scale of cislunar space, and the visual reality of standing on the moon through primary source photographs. This is not an illustrated book about space. It is space, documented by the people who went there.
hook: 129 NASA photographs from the Apollo missions. No illustrations. No filters. The actual moon.

title: Gentle Ben
author: Walt Morey
orbital_description: The book opens Alaskan coastal ecology, brown bear biology, the fishing economy of Southeast Alaska, and the ethics of keeping wild animals. The terrain and weather are accurately rendered throughout.
hook: A boy in Alaska befriends a bear chained in a shed — and the friendship teaches him and everyone around him something about wildness and trust.

title: George and Martha
author: James Marshall
orbital_description: The stories are tiny ethics lessons: what to do when your friend hates your cooking, when you discover something private, when someone doesn't want your help. Each one opens a real friendship dilemma with humor and warmth.
hook: Five short stories about two hippos who are best friends — and who test every assumption about what friendship requires.

title: Gigi and Ojiji
author: Melissa Iwai
orbital_description: The 2023 Geisel Honor book opens intergenerational relationships across a language barrier, the concept of nonverbal communication, and the specific texture of a grandparent-grandchild bond that doesn't depend on shared vocabulary. The early reader format makes it accessible while the emotional content is genuinely deep.
hook: Gigi doesn't speak her grandfather's language — but they find a way to say everything that matters without words.

title: Go Tell It: The Story of Nat Turner's Rebellion
author: Quartez Harris
heads_up: Depicts slavery and rebellion violence; discuss before reading.
orbital_description: 2026 Sibert Honor Book for a 2025 publication. Opens the history of Nat Turner's 1831 rebellion in Southampton County, Virginia, the geography of the antebellum Virginia Tidewater, and the long argument in American history about how to remember and teach this event. Gordon C. James's art is painted with the weight of the subject.
hook: Nat Turner led the most significant slave rebellion in American history in 1831 — and this picture book opens one of the most contested events in the story of American freedom.

title: Golden Gate: Building the Mighty Bridge
author: Elizabeth Partridge
orbital_description: ALSC 2026 Notable Recordings (implies 2025 book pub). Elizabeth Partridge (Seen and Unseen, 2023 Sibert Medal) opens the engineering of suspension bridges through the specific design challenges of the Golden Gate, the labor history of the construction workers including the safety net that saved 19 lives, the geography of the San Francisco Bay, and the Depression-era economics of public works.
hook: Building the Golden Gate Bridge required solving engineering problems nobody had ever faced before — and doing it with the lives of workers who had no safety net except for a revolutionary net hung below.

title: Good Golden Sun
author: Brendan Wenzel
orbital_description: SLJ Best Picture Book 2025. Brendan Wenzel (Hello, I'm Here!; They All Saw a Cat, Caldecott Honor) opens solar energy as the foundation of all ecosystems through the visual language of energy flow — from sun to plant to insect to bird to decomposer — in a circular narrative that models photosynthesis and the food web.
hook: The sun's energy flows through an entire ecosystem in one day — from the top of the food chain to the bottom and back — in a book that is visually unlike anything else.

title: Goodnight Gorilla
author: Peggy Rathmann
orbital_description: The wordless-adjacent format requires children to narrate from visual cues, building storytelling and sequencing skills. The parade of animals introduces zoo species; the zookeeper's wife's discovery is a perfect setup-and-reveal.
hook: A gorilla steals the zookeeper's keys and sets every animal free for the night — told almost entirely in pictures.

title: Goodnight Moon
author: Margaret Wise Brown
orbital_description: Every object in the room becomes a vocabulary lesson; every repetition builds phonological awareness; the moon outside the window opens a first conversation about astronomy and the natural world.
hook: The most soothing two minutes in childhood — and a secret lesson in language pattern and the night sky.

title: Grand Canyon
author: Jason Chin
orbital_description: Jason Chin walks a father and daughter down into the canyon, and with every layer they walk backward in time.
hook: Every layer of rock in the Grand Canyon is a chapter of Earth's autobiography, and the oldest is two billion years old.

title: Grandmother's Dreamcatcher
author: Becky Ray McCain
orbital_description: Chippewa tradition, intergenerational relationships, sleep science, and material craft.
hook: A boy who cannot sleep learns that the web above his bed is not decoration. It is a filter for dreams.

title: Green Eggs and Ham
author: Dr. Seuss
orbital_description: The book uses exactly 50 words as a deliberate vocabulary constraint to build phonemic awareness; the persuasion structure models rhetorical persistence; the green eggs resolution teaches the cost of refusing to try anything unfamiliar.
hook: Sam-I-Am will not stop asking — and his relentless persuasion campaign is secretly a phonics lesson and an argument for open-mindedness.

title: Greenglass House
author: Kate Milford
heads_up: Adoption explored with emotional complexity.
orbital_description: Mystery, cartography, stained-glass chemistry, adoption identity, and tabletop RPG mechanics.
hook: A smugglers' inn on a cliff above a frozen river fills with mysterious guests during Christmas break.

title: Grief Is an Elephant
author: Tamara Ellis Smith
heads_up: Designed for children processing loss; discuss before reading if needed.
orbital_description: The book opens grief as a physical, spatial experience through extended metaphor, the psychology of loss and its non-linear timeline, and the way that acknowledging something enormous is the first step toward living alongside it. Whitesides's illustrations render invisible weight with stunning visual precision.
hook: Grief takes up space, makes noise, gets in the way — and this book gives it exactly the right shape.

title: Growing Vegetable Soup
author: Lois Ehlert
orbital_description: Each vegetable is labeled in both English and Spanish; the planting sequence follows real agricultural timing; the cooking process introduces kitchen science. This is nutrition, botany, and food systems in a single arc.
hook: A parent and child grow every vegetable in their soup from seed to pot — the farm-to-table story at its most elemental.

title: Grumpy Monkey
author: Suzanne Lang
orbital_description: The book validates the full range of emotions including the right to feel bad without explanation. Grumpy Monkey pushes back against forced positivity with humor, modeling emotional self-awareness and the value of simply sitting with a feeling.
hook: Jim Panzee does not want to be happy today — and no amount of cheerful advice will change that.

title: Guts
author: Raina Telgemeier
orbital_description: Opens the neuroscience of the gut-brain axis through Raina's experience of anxiety manifesting as gastrointestinal symptoms, the concept of a phobia vs. a preference through her food avoidance, and the therapy process from a child's perspective. The graphic memoir format makes interior states visible in ways prose cannot.
hook: Raina woke up one morning with a terrible stomach ache — and the stomach ache never really went away. This is the graphic memoir of what anxiety feels like from the inside.

title: Hair Love
author: Matthew A. Cherry
heads_up: The reason Mom is absent is revealed at the end.
orbital_description: Texture science, problem-solving, Black hair culture, and fatherhood as practice. Academy Award-winning short film origin.
hook: Daddy has never done Zuri's hair before. He watches tutorials, breaks a comb, and makes a mess. Then he gets it right.

title: Harold and the Purple Crayon
author: Crockett Johnson
orbital_description: The book models creative agency and design thinking: Harold identifies a problem, generates a solution, adjusts when it fails, and tries again. The entire story is a sequence of invention loops children absorb before they can name the process.
hook: Harold draws his entire world into existence with one crayon — and when something goes wrong, he draws a solution.

title: Harriet the Spy
author: Louise Fitzhugh
orbital_description: The book opens the ethics of observation and private writing, the social costs of honesty without kindness, New York City's Upper East Side geography, and the question of what separates a scientist from a snoop.
hook: Harriet writes everything she observes about everyone in her notebook — until the notebook is found.

title: Hatchet
author: Gary Paulsen
heads_up: Divorce. Plane crash. Moose attack.
orbital_description: Every chapter is a survival problem that requires real science to solve. Newbery Honor.
hook: A thirteen-year-old crashes alone in the Canadian wilderness with nothing but a hatchet and a secret about his parents.

title: Hatchet
author: Gary Paulsen
heads_up: Depicts a plane crash (pilot dies of heart attack), vomiting, near-starvation, and ongoing emotional distress from parental divorce. Age-appropriate for 8+ but intense.
orbital_description: Brian Robeson's bush plane crashes in the Canadian backcountry, and the only tool he has is a hatchet. Gary Paulsen builds survival as applied science: fire from sparks and birch bark, shelter from wind direction, fishing from patience and observation, berry identification from trial and painful error. The child absorbs botany, zoology, weather prediction, tool physics, and the psychology of isolation. The divorce subplot runs underneath, giving the survival story emotional stakes beyond the physical. Every chapter teaches a skill the child can test.
hook: A thirteen-year-old boy, a hatchet, and fifty-four days alone in the Canadian wilderness.

title: Hattie Mae Begins Again
author: Sharon G. Flake
orbital_description: Pub January 13, 2026. Sharon Flake (The Skin I'm In, Coretta Scott King Author Award) opens the historical geography of the Great Migration and Black boarding school culture in 1930s America, the class dynamics within Black communities during the Depression, and the social-emotional architecture of belonging in a space where every signal says you don't fit.
hook: Hattie Mae is 12 years old, from a poor rural community, and suddenly at Miss Abigail's School for Exceptional Young Ladies in 1938 — feeling like a 'country bumpkin straight off the turnip truck' next to girls who have had everything.

title: Hello, Stranger
author: Barbara O'Connor
orbital_description: The book opens rural North Carolina geography and the social texture of small-town life, the biology of stray dog behavior, and the mechanics of friendship between two people who don't know how to start one. O'Connor's regional setting is rendered with the specificity of someone who knows the landscape intimately.
hook: Two lonely kids in a small North Carolina town connect over a stray dog neither of them is allowed to keep.

title: Henry, Like Always
author: Jenn Bailey
orbital_description: The 2024 Geisel Honor book opens predictable sequence structure as both reading skill and emotional anchor: children who need routine will recognize Henry's anxiety when it breaks. The early reader format uses repetition to build both literacy and procedural comfort.
hook: Henry's school day follows the exact same sequence every day — and the day it doesn't is the whole story.

title: Hidden Figures Young Readers' Edition
author: Margot Lee Shetterly
orbital_description: Opens the mathematics of orbital mechanics through Katherine Johnson's calculations for John Glenn's Friendship 7 mission, the history of NASA from NACA through the space race, the geography of Hampton, Virginia as the site of Langley Research Center, and the social history of segregated federal employment. Written by a journalist who grew up in the same community.
hook: Before NASA had computers, it had human computers — women who did the math by hand. The ones nobody wrote about were Black, and they were brilliant.

title: Hilo: The Boy Who Crashed to Earth
author: Judd Winick
orbital_description: Opens physics through Hilo's energy mechanics and the science of interplanetary travel, the concept of identity as constructed vs. discovered through a protagonist with amnesia, and the friendship dynamic of two kids with completely different personalities who protect each other's weaknesses. The humor is precise and the emotional payoff is earned.
hook: A boy from another planet crash-lands on Earth with no idea where he came from — and his new best friend has to help him figure out what he is before the things chasing him arrive.

title: Holding Her Own: The Exceptional Life of Jackie Ormes
author: Traci N. Todd
orbital_description: The 2024 Sibert Honor book opens the history of newspaper syndication, the specific restrictions on Black journalists and cartoonists in the 1930s-1950s, the civil rights advocacy embedded in Ormes's cartoon characters, and Wright's sequential art technique as a model of the same craft Ormes pioneered.
hook: Jackie Ormes was the first Black woman to publish a nationally syndicated cartoon strip — and she used it to say everything the newspapers wouldn't print.

title: Holes
author: Louis Sachar
orbital_description: Newbery Medal 1999. Opens the geology and ecology of the Texas desert through the lake-that-once-was and the yellow-spotted lizard, the history of the American frontier through the Kissing Kate Barlow backstory, and the concept of fate through a plot that braids three separate timelines into a single knot that only untangles at the end.
hook: Stanley Yelnats is sent to a juvenile camp in the Texas desert where the boys dig holes all day — and the reason for the holes connects three storylines across 110 years.

title: Honeybird Blue
author: Taunya English
orbital_description: PW Spring 2026 Sneak Preview. Opens the ornithology of bird coloration including the biology of feather pigmentation and structural color, the ecology of local bird habitats, and the social-emotional architecture of a parent-child nature walk as a form of intergenerational attention.
hook: A girl and her father go on a nature walk to find the many colors of birds — and the walk is about far more than birds.

title: Hot Dog
author: Doug Salati
orbital_description: The book opens urban ecology, city geography, heat science, and the sensory experience of a summer day through a dog's nose and paws. Salati's pastel watercolor illustrations earned the Caldecott Medal for a reason: every spread is a lesson in light, color, and temperature. After this one, children notice what hot pavement feels like on bare feet.
hook: A city dog on the hottest day of summer finds his way to the shore — and the whole arc of relief is something any child who has ever been too warm will understand.

title: How a Bear Became a Book: The Collaboration That Created Winnie-the-Pooh
author: Annette Bay Pimentel
orbital_description: Multiple starred reviews from MacKids 2026 roundup. Opens the natural history of bears, the WWI history behind A.A. Milne's military service and the bear named after Winnipeg, the craft of picture book collaboration between writer and illustrator, and the history of British children's literature in the early 20th century. From the author of Before Colors.
hook: Before Winnie-the-Pooh was a book, he was a real bear at the London Zoo — and the collaboration between A.A. Milne and E.H. Shepard created one of the most beloved children's books in history.

title: How a House Is Built
author: Gail Gibbons
orbital_description: The book tracks a full construction sequence: surveying, foundation, framing, roofing, plumbing, electrical, and finishing. Each trade is named and visualized. Children who read this look at every building they enter differently.
hook: Every step of building a house from blueprint to moving day — clear, accurate, and illustrated like a technical diagram a child can love.

title: How Dinosaurs Went Extinct: A Safety Guide
author: Ame Dyckman
orbital_description: The book opens paleontology and mass extinction science through a comedy premise: each dinosaur species died because it ignored a specific safety guideline. Children absorb real extinction causes including the Chicxulub asteroid, climate change, and food chain collapse while laughing at absurd safety posters.
hook: Dinosaurs went extinct because they didn't follow basic safety rules — and the evidence is presented with courtroom seriousness.

title: How Do Dinosaurs Say Goodnight
author: Jane Yolen
orbital_description: Every dinosaur is real and named: Stegosaurus, Ankylosaurus, Tyrannosaurus Rex. The bad bedtime behavior maps to real child behavior; the eventual compliance is reassuring. Children absorb dinosaur taxonomy through a bedtime routine.
hook: Real dinosaur species have bedtime tantrums — and then, eventually, they go to sleep just like children.

title: How Many Seeds in a Pumpkin?
author: Margaret McNamara
orbital_description: Margaret McNamara sets this in a classroom where Mr. Tiffin brings three pumpkins and asks: how many seeds? The biggest kid picks the biggest pumpkin. The smallest kid, Charlie, picks the smallest one. The class counts by 2s, 5s, and 10s. The smallest pumpkin has the most seeds, and the book explains why (more segments, more seeds per segment, botany determines count, not size). G. Brian Karas' illustrations carry the classroom math process while the text carries the botanical science. The child absorbs skip-counting, estimation, pumpkin biology, and the principle that assumptions about size are often wrong.
hook: Three pumpkins, three sizes. The smallest kid guesses the most seeds. He is right, and the reason is the math.

title: How to Be a Good Creature: A Memoir in Thirteen Animals
author: Sy Montgomery
orbital_description: Sibert Honor 2019. Opens the ethology (animal behavior science) of 13 species through Montgomery's field encounters as a naturalist, the philosophy of what relationships between humans and animals can teach about consciousness and emotion, and the memoir form as a scientific narrative tool. Montgomery is one of the great science writers for children.
hook: Thirteen animals — pigs, octopuses, emus, tarantulas — each one teaching Sy Montgomery something essential about being alive that she could not have learned from a human.

title: How to Count to 1
author: Casper Salmon
orbital_description: The book teaches the concept of sets, cardinality, and visual counting through deliberate absurdity: every instruction to count one thing is illustrated with a spread that contains multiple examples of that thing. Children must find and verify the single correct answer while laughing at the wrong ones. Mathematical literacy as comedy.
hook: The instructions are simple: count to one. No higher. The math and the comedy are perfectly balanced.

title: How to Find a Fox
author: Nilah Magruder
orbital_description: Nilah Magruder sends a Black girl into the forest with a field guide, a backpack, and a plan. The fox never appears. What appears instead is the entire woodland ecosystem: tracks, scat, burrows, birdsong, lichen, mushrooms. The child absorbs tracking skills, woodland ecology, and the scientific principle that failed experiments still produce data. The girl is not disappointed. She is loaded with evidence. She will come back tomorrow.
hook: She goes into the woods to find a fox. She does not find one. She finds everything else.

title: How to Hatch: A Gosling's Guide to Breaking Free
author: Sara Holly Ackerman
orbital_description: PW starred. Opens egg biology including air cell development, the pip reflex, and the mechanics of breaking through a shell, presented as a first-person narration that simultaneously describes the science and models persistence under difficulty. The dual function is remarkably clean.
hook: A gosling inside an egg narrates the exact scientific process of hatching — and the pep talk she gives herself along the way is both biologically accurate and genuinely encouraging.

title: How to Write a Poem
author: Kwame Alexander
orbital_description: The book opens the craft of poetry through the instruction itself becoming the poem: each page demonstrates the technique it describes. Sweet's mixed-media collage illustrations model the same aesthetic principle — found materials assembled into something beautiful. Children who read this write differently.
hook: Everything you need to write a poem is already around you — and this book shows you exactly where to look.

title: Howl's Moving Castle
author: Diana Wynne Jones
orbital_description: Opens the fairy tale tradition through radical subversion of the eldest-daughter-must-fail narrative, the geography and folklore of a fantasy Wales, the philosophy of identity through a protagonist who discovers herself by being forced to be old, and Diana Wynne Jones's signature misdirection comedy. The Miyazaki film is good; the book is richer.
hook: Sophie is cursed to be an old woman and walks into the moving castle of the most dreadful wizard in the land — who turns out to be very different from what everyone says.

title: Human Body Theater
author: Maris Wicks
heads_up: Includes age-appropriate coverage of the reproductive system. Factual, not graphic.
orbital_description: Maris Wicks uses the graphic novel format to teach every major body system: skeletal, muscular, circulatory, respiratory, digestive, nervous, endocrine, immune, reproductive. The skeleton is the narrator and tour guide, breaking the fourth wall constantly. The comic panel format carries cellular biology, organ function, and systemic interaction visually, which means the child absorbs anatomy through sequential art rather than textbook diagrams. The humor is anatomically accurate. The science is rigorous. The child finishes this knowing more about their own body than most adults.
hook: A skeleton hosts a guided tour of the human body. Every system gets its own act. The audience is the reader's own organs.

title: I Am Malala (Young Readers Edition)
author: Malala Yousafzai with Patricia McCormick
orbital_description: Opens the history and geography of Pakistan's Swat Valley through Malala's childhood landscape, the history of the Taliban and its specific prohibitions in the Swat Valley, the neuroscience of traumatic brain injury through her recovery, and the concept of courage as an ongoing choice rather than a single moment.
hook: At 15, Malala was shot in the head on her school bus by the Taliban because she had been publicly advocating for girls' right to education. She survived and kept talking.

title: I Did It!
author: Michael Emberley
orbital_description: The 2023 Geisel Award winner captures the specific triumph of early childhood independence: the accomplishment is tiny, the pride is enormous, and that gap is the lesson. Simple sentences and repetition build early reading stamina while the emotional content models self-efficacy.
hook: A little bear finally does something all by himself — and the brimming pride is completely out of proportion to the task, which is exactly right.

title: I Hear the Snow, I Smell the Sea
author: Janice Milusich
orbital_description: 2026 Schneider Family Book Award Honor (young children). Opens sensory neuroscience through the specific experience of a child who perceives the environment differently, the physics of sound in winter landscapes, and the chemistry of ocean smell (dimethyl sulfide from marine algae). Chris Raschka's expressionist art style translates sensation into visual form. NOTE: Likely pub 2025 — verify before placing on 2026 shelf.
hook: A child experiences snow and sea through sound and smell rather than sight — and the world opens differently when you experience it through different senses.

title: I Like Myself!
author: Karen Beaumont
orbital_description: A book about selfhood that sneaks in rhyme, rhythm, and body vocabulary without ever feeling like a lesson.
hook: A girl with wild curls declares everything she loves about herself and dares the world to disagree.

title: I Will Always Write Back: How One Letter Changed Two Lives
author: Caitlin Alifirenka and Martin Ganda with Liz Welch
orbital_description: Opens the geography and economic history of Zimbabwe through Martin's descriptions of daily life under hyperinflation and poverty, the history of the pen pal tradition as an educational practice, and the social-emotional concept of correspondence as a form of intimacy that crosses radical inequality.
hook: A girl from Pennsylvania and a boy from Zimbabwe started writing to each other as a sixth-grade assignment — and the exchange revealed two completely different worlds.

title: If You Give a Mouse a Cookie
author: Laura Numeroff
orbital_description: The circular structure ending where it began models mathematical cycles and logical sequence. Each link in the chain is a prediction waiting to be made: what comes next? Children learn to trace consequences forward and backward.
hook: One cookie triggers an unstoppable chain reaction — the funniest math lesson about cause and effect ever written.

title: If You Give a Pig a Pancake
author: Laura Numeroff
orbital_description: This companion book extends the cause-and-effect model while introducing where maple syrup comes from, cooking chemistry, and the natural source of pancake ingredients. The sequence layers math onto food systems.
hook: A pig's pancake breakfast spirals into a day of cause-and-effect chaos — and along the way introduces maple syrup production and food science.

title: If You Made a Million
author: David M. Schwartz
orbital_description: The book that makes money tangible. Real math, real banking concepts, wild visual comparisons.
hook: A magical mathematician shows kids what a million dollars actually looks like, and how a penny becomes one.

title: If You Plant a Seed
author: Kadir Nelson
orbital_description: Nearly wordless. Plant growth cycles, soil and water requirements, and social concepts of generosity.
hook: Plant a seed of selfishness and watch what grows. Plant a seed of kindness and watch what grows.

title: Iggy Peck, Architect
author: Andrea Beaty
orbital_description: Engineering, art history, and physics wrapped in rhyming verse. Illustrations reference real landmarks.
hook: A boy who has been building things since he was two constructs a bridge out of shoes and string.

title: Impossible Escape: A True Story of Survival and Heroism in Nazi Europe
author: Steve Sheinkin
heads_up: Contains detailed descriptions of the Holocaust.
orbital_description: Sydney Taylor Book Award Honor, SLJ Best Book 2023. Opens the history of Auschwitz-Birkenau through the escape plan's internal map of the camp's geography, the specific timeline of the Hungarian deportations, the physics and chemistry of the escape method, and the political history of why Allied governments received but did not act on the Vrba-Wetzler Report.
hook: Rudolf Vrba escaped from Auschwitz — one of fewer than 700 people who ever did — and raced to get the truth to the world before Hungary's 750,000 Jews were deported there to die.

title: In a Small Small Pond
author: Denise Fleming
orbital_description: Every creature named is real: heron, tadpole, dragonfly, turtle. The seasonal arc from spring to winter maps a full ecological cycle. The rhyming couplets make the vocabulary stick in ways prose cannot.
hook: A year at a small pond through the eyes of the creatures who live there — rhyme and pulp-paper art make ecology unforgettable.

title: In Every Life
author: Marla Frazee
heads_up: The book depicts death as part of the life cycle; discuss before reading if needed.
orbital_description: Without a single word, the book maps the full biological life cycle across species, seasonal change, community formation, and intergenerational continuity. Frazee's crosshatched ink illustrations received the Caldecott Honor for their visual precision. Children and adults read the same book and see different things in it.
hook: A wordless journey through a human life and an animal life running parallel — and the final pages will stop you completely.

title: In the Desert
author: David Elliott
orbital_description: Confirmed March 18, 2025. David Elliott and Gordy Wright open Saharan desert ecology through the specific organisms that survive in one of Earth's most extreme environments, the geography of the Sahara as an ecosystem rather than an absence, and the literary technique of a poem as a concentrated lens on natural observation.
hook: Poems and art pay tribute to the Sahara's abundant flora and fauna — a reminder that the world's largest desert is teeming with life.

title: In the Desert: Poems of the Sahara
author: David Elliott
heads_up: NOTE: This duplicates entry #17 (In the Desert) in this batch. Claude Code should deduplicate before import.
orbital_description: Confirmed March 18, 2025. This standalone listing provides the correct title. Opens Saharan ecology through the lives of animals that survive in one of Earth's most extreme environments — the fennec fox's ears, the sidewinder's diagonal track, the date palm's root system — each rendered in a poem that doubles as biological observation.
hook: Poems and art that prove the Sahara is teeming with life — and that a desert poem can be as precise as a field guide.

title: Inch by Inch
author: Leo Lionni
orbital_description: The book introduces linear measurement and the concept of a standard unit through a story set in a real bird habitat. Each creature the inchworm measures is identifiable by field guide; the math is embedded in survival. After this one, children start measuring things around the house.
hook: An inchworm survives by making himself useful — and every bird he measures is a real species with a real wingspan.

title: Inkheart
author: Cornelia Funke
orbital_description: Opens the craft of reading aloud and oral storytelling traditions, the geography of northern Italy through the family's hiding places, the concept of fiction as a world with internal logical rules that continue when characters leave it, and the literary history of the book-within-a-book structure.
hook: A man who can read characters out of books accidentally read a villain out of a novel 12 years ago — and now his daughter Meggie discovers she has the same power.

title: Invisible Things
author: Andy J. Pizza
orbital_description: The book opens physics through invisible forces including gravity and magnetism and sound waves, biology through the invisible processes of growth and digestion, and philosophy through the invisible qualities of friendship and love. Pizza's visual design makes abstract science tangible.
hook: Everything important is invisible — and this book makes the invisible visible, one page at a time.

title: Is It Spring?
author: Kevin Henkes
orbital_description: Kirkus Spring 2026 Preview standout. Opens seasonal change through the specific meteorological uncertainty of late winter and early spring, the emotional vocabulary of waiting and disappointment and eventual joy, and the natural history observation of what spring actually looks and feels like when it arrives. Henkes (Lily's Purple Plastic Purse, Caldecott Medal for Kitten's First Full Moon) at his most precise.
hook: Spring announces itself haltingly — snow one day, buds the next — and the book captures the specific emotional experience of waiting for the season to finally arrive.

title: Ish
author: Peter Reynolds
orbital_description: The book dismantles perfectionism by naming the alternative: ish. A vase-ish picture. A tree-ish picture. The concept gives children permission to make art that isn't finished or correct, which is where all real creative work begins.
hook: Ramon gives up drawing after his brother laughs — until his sister shows him a wall full of his ish drawings she has saved.

title: Island of the Blue Dolphins
author: Scott O'Dell
orbital_description: Newbery Medal 1961. Based on the true story of the Lone Woman of San Nicolas Island. Opens Chumash culture and the specific ecology of the Channel Islands, the California history of indigenous displacement through the mission system, Pacific marine biology through Karana's fishing and sea otter interactions, and the psychology of long-term solitude.
hook: Karana is left behind when her people evacuate from a remote Pacific island — and survives alone for 18 years, becoming one of the most extraordinary figures in California history.

title: Island Storm
author: Brian Floca
orbital_description: The Kirkus Prize Finalist opens meteorology through a coastal storm system including atmospheric pressure changes visible as darkening sky and wind shifts, the geography of a small seaside town, and the developmental psychology of exploring boundaries. Floca (Locomotive, Caldecott Medal) and Smith (Sidewalk Flowers, Caldecott Honor) together are at the top of their field.
hook: A storm rolls in and two children explore their seaside town farther and farther from home — a picture book about weather science and the courage to go a little farther.

title: Iveliz Explains It All
author: Andrea Beatriz Arango
orbital_description: The 2023 Newbery Honor verse novel opens ADHD neurobiology through the specific cognitive experience of a Puerto Rican American girl in middle school, the social dynamics of neurodiversity in the classroom, and family systems under stress. The verse format models how poetic compression captures a restless mind.
hook: Iveliz has ADHD and a lot to explain — and her explanations in verse are funnier and sharper than anyone gives her credit for.

title: J vs. K
author: Jerry Craft and Kwame Alexander
orbital_description: The book opens the creative process of both writing and illustration through a story about students discovering their creative identities. The hybrid format models both prose writing and sequential art, making the craft visible while also delivering a genuine story about competition and collaboration.
hook: Two Newbery Medal winners co-write a book about two fifth-graders with their own creative rivalry — and the book teaches you how to write and draw yours.

title: Jovita Wore Pants: The Story of a Mexican Freedom Fighter
author: Aida Salazar
orbital_description: The 2024 Caldecott Honor book opens Mexican Revolutionary history, the journalism of a woman who used words as weapons before she picked up a gun, and the specific courage of crossing gender boundaries in wartime. Mendoza's striking illustration style earned the Caldecott Honor.
hook: Jovita Idár disguised herself as a man to fight in the Mexican Revolution — and her journalism and medicine saved more lives than her rifle.

title: Judge Ketanji Brown Jackson: A Life Committed to Justice
author: Lori Rozsa and Garen Thomas
orbital_description: PW starred. Opens the history of the Supreme Court as an institution, the civil rights milestones embedded in Jackson's biography, the geography of Miami's Liberty City and HBCU culture, and the specific legal cases that formed her judicial philosophy. Interwoven historical context makes the biography a history of American law.
hook: Ketanji Brown Jackson became the first Black woman confirmed to the US Supreme Court — and her path there is a history of American civil rights law told through one person's life.

title: Jumanji
author: Chris Van Allsburg
orbital_description: The book models consequence-based thinking — every choice has a result you cannot undo — while introducing African savanna animals and their actual behaviors. The instructions matter: rules in Jumanji are the rules of cause and effect.
hook: Two bored children start a board game and discover every move releases a real jungle animal into their house.

title: Jumper: A Day in the Life of a Backyard Jumping Spider
author: Jessica Lanan
orbital_description: The book opens arachnid biology including the unique forward-facing eyes of jumping spiders that give them near-human visual acuity, predation strategies, silk production, and the specific microhabitat of a backyard. The back matter includes range maps, species identification, and scientific methodology. After this one, children stop squashing jumping spiders.
hook: A jumping spider's day is a hunt, a leap, and a study in eight-eyed perception — and the illustrations are close enough to make you flinch.

title: Just in Case: Saving Seeds in the Svalbard Global Seed Vault
author: Megan Clendenan
heads_up: NOTE: Published Oct 2025. Included here because 2026 Orbis Pictus Award. Verify placement in library vs. New and Notable.
orbital_description: 2026 Orbis Pictus Award Winner, 2026 CBC Outstanding Science Trade Books and Best STEM Books. Starred reviews from SLJ, Horn Book, and Shelf Awareness. Opens the biology of seed preservation and genetic diversity, the Arctic geography of Svalbard, the global cooperation required to maintain the vault, and the ecology of food security as a scientific challenge.
hook: Deep in an Arctic Norwegian mountain is a vault holding seeds from every food crop on Earth — a backup for civilization's food supply, built against the possibility that everything goes wrong.

title: Knuffle Bunny
author: Mo Willems
orbital_description: The book captures the exact experience of pre-verbal communication and the desperation of not being understood. The Brooklyn neighborhood photographs anchor the story in real geography; the dialogue between father and toddler is phonologically precise.
hook: Trixie can't yet speak — but she knows the laundromat has her bunny, and getting the message across is the whole emergency.

title: Legendary Frybread Drive-In: Intertribal Stories
author: edited by Cynthia Leitich Smith
orbital_description: The Printz Award winner opens the geography and political history of Native American tribal sovereignty, the concept of intertribal as distinct from a single indigenous identity, the diverse literary voices of Native American authors writing across genre, and the social-emotional experience of Native teenagers navigating two worlds. The title comes from a real place that is a community gathering point.
hook: Indigenous authors from across more than a dozen nations tell the stories of Native American teenagers — and the anthology's title alone teaches that Native American culture is not one thing, it is hundreds.

title: Leo the Late Bloomer
author: Robert Kraus
orbital_description: The book is a gentle intervention in developmental anxiety, assuring children and parents alike that development is not a race. The father who watches and worries models parental anxiety; Leo's eventual bloom models developmental confidence.
hook: Leo can't read or draw or eat neatly — until one day he can, and the whole jungle breathes out.

title: Life after Whale
author: Lynn Brunelle
orbital_description: The 2025 Sibert Medal winner opens deep ocean ecology through the process of whale fall, the specific organisms that depend on whale carcasses including hagfish and sleeper sharks and bone worms, the chemistry of sulfide-based ecosystems, and the philosophical point that death in nature is never a true ending. Chin's illustrations bring the deep sea to light.
hook: When a blue whale dies and sinks, her body becomes the richest real estate in the deep ocean — and the ecosystem that grows from it takes 100 years to complete.

title: Lilly's Purple Plastic Purse
author: Kevin Henkes
orbital_description: The book moves through impulse, action, consequence, regret, repair, and forgiveness in ten pages, mapping the full arc of a relational rupture and reconciliation. Writing the note makes literacy feel emotionally consequential.
hook: Lilly adores her teacher until he takes her purse away — and then she writes him a note she immediately regrets.

title: Lincoln's Grave Robbers
author: Steve Sheinkin
orbital_description: Opens the history of the Secret Service (originally created to combat counterfeiting, not protect presidents), the chemistry of 19th-century counterfeiting through the specific forging methods used, the history of Lincoln's burial and challenges of preserving his remains, and the concept of a heist story where the target is the body of a president.
hook: A gang of counterfeiters plots to steal Abraham Lincoln's body from his Springfield tomb in 1876 — and the Secret Service has to stop them without Lincoln's family finding out.

title: Little Bear
author: Else Holmelund Minarik
orbital_description: Else Holmelund Minarik wrote one of the first true early readers: controlled vocabulary, repeating structures, and emotional depth that treats the beginning reader as a real person. Maurice Sendak's crosshatched illustrations carry the seasons and a bear's woodland habitat while the four linked stories carry imagination (flying to the moon), material science (making soup from nothing), birthday customs, and the unshakeable security of a parent who is always present. The child reads this independently and absorbs reading fluency, story structure, and the idea that books reward you for the work of decoding them.
hook: Little Bear wants to fly to the moon. His mother says he cannot. He tries anyway. She is waiting when he lands.

title: Little Blue Truck
author: Alice Schertle
orbital_description: The book models the social return on investment of kindness: Blue's habit of greeting animals pays dividends when he needs help. Farm animal names and sounds are a bonus biology lesson.
hook: Little Blue Truck greets every farm animal by name and gets stuck in the mud — and every friend he made rushes to push him free.

title: Little House in the Big Woods
author: Laura Ingalls Wilder
orbital_description: The book opens food preservation including smoking meat and making butter, seasonal farming, Wisconsin wilderness ecology, and the social fabric of isolated frontier communities. Children discover that most things they take for granted were once made by hand.
hook: A year in a Wisconsin log cabin in 1870 — and every detail of how pioneer families made everything they needed from scratch.

title: Little Rebels
author: Yuyi Morales
orbital_description: The NYT Best Illustrated 2025 winner and Caldecott and Pura Belpré Honor author's latest opens the history of child and youth activism across cultures and centuries, the concept of civil resistance as a historical constant, and the social-emotional idea that children have been agents of change in their own right across history.
hook: From the creator of Dreamers — a book about the rebels throughout history who changed the world, starting with the littlest ones.

title: Lola
author: Karla Arenas Valenti
orbital_description: The 2025 Belpré Award winner weaves Mexican folk tradition into a modern fable about desire, consequence, and the meaning of knowing yourself. The dueling spirit structure maps a genuine choice architecture that children experience daily in smaller form.
hook: Lola must make a choice between two spirits — one offering everything she wants, one asking for something she doesn't yet understand.

title: Loops
author: Jashar Awan
orbital_description: Most anticipated 2026 from multiple lists. From the Caldecott Honor and Geisel Honor creator of Every Monday Mabel (2026 Caldecott Honor). Opens the concept of visual language — how shape and line communicate emotion — the mathematics of circles and spirals in geometry, and the social-emotional vocabulary of a child moving through feelings they are just learning to name.
hook: A story told almost entirely through circles, loops, and spirals — each shape carrying the emotional weight of loneliness, frustration, and joy without a word needing to do the work.

title: Mae and Gerty and the Matter with Matter
author: Elaine Vickers
orbital_description: The book opens the three states of matter through the relationship between two opposite sisters: one orderly and solid, one scattered and gaseous. The metaphor is scientifically accurate and emotionally precise. Children absorb the properties of solids, liquids, and gases through a sibling story they recognize.
hook: Two sisters are as different as a solid and a gas — and their chemistry is the lesson.

title: Maizy Chen's Last Chance
author: Lisa Yee
orbital_description: The 2023 Newbery Honor book opens Chinese American immigration history through the specific lens of a family restaurant, small-town California geography, and the secrets families keep across generations. Every clue Maizy finds is a lesson in American history she was never taught in school.
hook: Maizy's family restaurant in a small California town holds a mystery — and uncovering it means understanding who her family really is.

title: Make Way for Ducklings
author: Robert McCloskey
orbital_description: The book opens urban ecology, Boston's real geography and landmarks, community cooperation, and early problem-solving. The city children can later find on a globe is woven through every illustration.
hook: A mother duck navigates the city of Boston to find a safe home for her ducklings — and an entire city stops to help her do it.

title: Making Art
author: Diana Ejaita
orbital_description: The NYT Best Illustrated 2025 winner opens the history and geometry of West African textile design traditions including Kente and Kuba cloth, the concept of art-making as a mode of understanding the world, and the social-emotional value of the creative process. Ejaita's illustration style is immediately recognizable — rich pattern, warm color, geometric depth.
hook: Making art is making meaning — and Ejaita's visual language draws from West African textile tradition to show a child why the process matters more than the product.

title: Mama Miti: Wangari Maathai and the Trees of Kenya
author: Donna Jo Napoli
orbital_description: Environmental science, African geography, political history, and biography all in one picture book.
hook: A Kenyan woman plants one tree, then convinces an entire country to plant thirty million more.

title: Mama Panya's Pancakes
author: Mary Chamberlin & Rich Chamberlin
orbital_description: Mary and Rich Chamberlin set this in a Kenyan village where Adamu invites everyone he passes on the way to market for Mama Panya's pancakes. Swahili words are woven into the text. Julia Cairns' illustrations carry East African market culture, agricultural products, and village architecture. The child absorbs Swahili vocabulary, Kenyan daily life, counting and addition (how many guests now?), the economics of communal sharing, and the resolution: every guest brings something, and two pancakes become a feast. The math problem is real. The solution is social.
hook: Mama Panya has just enough flour for two pancakes. Her son Adamu invites the whole village. The math does not work. The community does.

title: Maniac Magee
author: Jerry Spinelli
orbital_description: Newbery Medal 1991. Opens the history of de facto racial segregation in Northern cities through the geography of Two Mills, Pennsylvania, the physics of long-distance running through Maniac's abilities, and the concept of a folk legend as a narrative device that elevates ordinary social reality to mythological scale.
hook: Jeffrey Magee runs so fast he becomes a legend — and runs across the invisible line dividing his Pennsylvania town into two worlds that have forgotten they were once one.

title: March: Book One
author: John Lewis and Andrew Aydin
orbital_description: Eisner Award winner. Opens the history of the civil rights movement from the ground level through Lewis's personal account of the lunch counter sit-ins, freedom rides, and nonviolent direct action training, the geography of the American South as a site of systematic racial violence, and the concept of nonviolent resistance as a deliberate and disciplined strategy rather than passivity.
hook: John Lewis tells the story of how he became a civil rights activist — from growing up in rural Alabama to joining the lunch counter sit-ins that shook the South.

title: Marie's Magic Eggs: How Marie Procai Kept the Ukrainian Art of Pysanky Alive
author: Sandra Neil Wallace
orbital_description: PW starred. Opens the ancient Ukrainian tradition of pysanky including the chemistry of beeswax resist and natural dyes, the geometric symbolism encoded in pysanky patterns as a form of written language, the history of Ukrainian immigration to America, and the concept of cultural preservation as an act of resistance.
hook: Pysanky are Ukrainian Easter eggs decorated with precise geometric patterns — an ancient art form that a woman named Marie Procai brought to America and refused to let disappear.

title: Maybe Something Beautiful
author: F. Isabel Campoy & Theresa Howell
orbital_description: Based on the real Urban Art Trail in San Diego's Barrio Logan, this book follows Mira as she meets a muralist and helps transform her gray neighborhood into an outdoor gallery. Rafael Lopez (who actually painted those real murals) illustrates the transformation with his signature saturated style. The child absorbs public art as civic participation, color theory, community organizing, and the idea that beauty is not a luxury but infrastructure. The gray-to-color visual arc teaches without narrating.
hook: A gray neighborhood. A girl with paint. A muralist who says yes. The walls come alive.

title: Menudo Sunday: A Spanglish Counting Book
author: María Dolores Águila
orbital_description: SLJ June 2025 starred review. Opens the mathematics of counting sets through the joyful structure of a Mexican family gathering, the cultural history of menudo as a Sunday morning tradition, and the concept of family as a counting exercise that keeps growing.
hook: Two abuelitos, three primos, four tías, and their dogs gather to make menudo on Sunday — and counting the family is as important as the recipe.

title: Messy Roots
author: Laura Gao
heads_up: Anti-Asian racism. Pandemic context.
orbital_description: Graphic memoir spanning Wuhan, Texas, and COVID-19. The pandemic chapter is the most honest account of anti-Asian violence for young readers.
hook: Laura Gao grew up caught between Wuhan and Texas, and then her hometown became the most hated word in America.

title: Mexikid
author: Pedro Martín
orbital_description: The 2024 Newbery Honor graphic memoir opens Mexican-American immigration history, the US-Mexico border geography, the dynamics of a very large family on a very long road trip, and the specific texture of a boy meeting his grandfather for the first time and recognizing himself.
hook: Pedro's family drives to Mexico to bring his grandfather back to the US — and the road trip is a full Mexican-American family history.

title: Mike Mulligan and His Steam Shovel
author: Virginia Lee Burton
orbital_description: This is an engineering story, a labor history, and a lesson about obsolescence and reinvention. Children encounter excavation, civic architecture, and the economics of technological change while cheering for a steam shovel.
hook: Mike and his steam shovel Mary Anne dig the town hall cellar in one day — and find that being useful takes many forms.

title: Mimic Makers: Biomimicry Inventors Inspired by Nature
author: Kristen Nordstrom
orbital_description: The book opens the field of biomimicry through specific inventors who solved engineering problems by studying natural structures, the biology of each creature whose design was borrowed, and the engineering process of translating a biological solution into a human application. After this one, children look at animals as engineering textbooks.
hook: Velcro came from a cocklebur. Bullet trains came from a kingfisher's beak. And the next great invention might come from a gecko's foot.

title: Miss Rumphius
author: Barbara Cooney
orbital_description: The book teaches botany including lupine seed dispersal, coastal Maine geography, ecology as personal contribution, and the concept of legacy — what you leave behind. After this one, every child notices wildflowers on roadsides.
hook: Alice Rumphius travels the world and then scatters lupine seeds across the Maine coast — and makes the whole coast more beautiful.

title: Mistaco
author: Eliza Kinkz
orbital_description: Kirkus Best of 2025 opens the social-emotional concept of grace in failure, Mexican-American food culture and the intergenerational kitchen ritual, and the specific comfort of being loved unconditionally within a family's food tradition. 'Mistaco' is a portmanteau of mistake and taco that perfectly describes a child who is trying hard and failing beautifully.
hook: A girl can't roll a round tortilla to save her life — and her abuelo will eat every single misshapen one.

title: Mixed: A Colorful Story
author: Arree Chung
orbital_description: Arree Chung personifies primary colors as communities that segregate by hue. Reds shout. Blues are cool. Yellows shine. They separate. Then a Red and a Yellow have an Orange child, and the boundaries dissolve. The child absorbs color theory (primary, secondary, tertiary mixing), the social mechanics of segregation and integration, and the art-science intersection of pigment behavior. The allegory works for toddlers as a color lesson and for older children as a community story. The science is real. The metaphor is earned.
hook: Reds, Yellows, and Blues live together. Then they decide they are different. A mixed-color child changes everything.

title: Moon Song
author: Michaela Goade
orbital_description: SLJ Best Picture Book 2025. Caldecott Honor illustrator Michaela Goade (Berry Song) opens Tlingit and Haida cosmology through the moon as a living presence in the forest and ocean, Pacific Northwest ecology and geography, and the concept of traditional cultural knowledge as a distinct way of understanding the natural world.
hook: The moon as seen from Tlingit and Haida tradition — lyrical, atmospheric, and steeped in the Pacific Northwest's living coastal culture.

title: Moonbird
author: Phillip Hoose
heads_up: Species decline. Some birds do not survive.
orbital_description: Tracks one banded bird across hemispheres. Migratory biology, horseshoe crab ecology, citizen science.
hook: A robin-sized bird named B95 has flown the distance from the Earth to the moon and back. Sibert Medal.

title: Most Dangerous: Daniel Ellsberg and the Secret History of the Vietnam War
author: Steve Sheinkin
orbital_description: YALSA Excellence in Nonfiction winner, National Book Award finalist. Opens the history of the Vietnam War through the Pentagon Papers' contents, the constitutional history of the First Amendment and press freedom through the Supreme Court case, and the psychology of whistleblowing through Ellsberg's transformation from government analyst to most dangerous man in America.
hook: Daniel Ellsberg had access to the most classified document in America — 7,000 pages of evidence that the government had been lying about Vietnam for 20 years — and had to decide what to do with it.

title: Mr. Popper's Penguins
author: Richard and Florence Atwater
orbital_description: The book opens Antarctic ecology, penguin biology including diet and breeding, the economics of a family on a tight budget, and the resourcefulness of turning a problem into an opportunity. The penguin act is a masterclass in necessity as invention.
hook: A house painter receives a live penguin from an Antarctic explorer — and twelve penguins later, the chaos becomes the solution.

title: Mr. Revere and I
author: Robert Lawson
orbital_description: The book opens the American Revolution through eyewitness animal perspective, maps the real Boston geography of the ride, and introduces the actual historical figures and their roles. The horse's aristocratic origin as a British mount is its own history lesson.
hook: Paul Revere's famous midnight ride — told entirely by his horse.

title: Mrs. Frisby and the Rats of NIMH
author: Robert C. O'Brien
orbital_description: Newbery Medal 1972. Opens the ethics of animal experimentation through the rats' history at the National Institute of Mental Health (a real institution), the neuroscience of intelligence enhancement, the concept of what a society would look like if built by escaped laboratory animals who learned to read, and the ecology of a farm as a site of coexisting species.
hook: A widowed mouse needs to save her sick son — and the only ones who can help are a colony of superintelligent rats who escaped from a government laboratory.

title: Mrs. Piggle-Wiggle
author: Betty MacDonald
orbital_description: The book is a gentle satire of parenting and child behavior: each chapter presents a specific problem and its consequence-based cure. The logic of each cure is internally consistent and scientifically funny.
hook: Mrs. Piggle-Wiggle's cures for bad behavior are absurd and perfect — and the children always learn the lesson they needed.

title: My Antarctica: True Adventures in the Land of Mummified Seals, Space Robots, and So Much More
author: G. Neri
orbital_description: The Kirkus Best of 2024 nonfiction title opens Antarctic geography and geology including the Dry Valleys and the ice sheet, climate science through field measurement, robotics through NASA's work on Antarctic analogs for Mars, and the personal transformation of a self-described science-avoider who discovers he's been a scientist all along.
hook: A writer who hated science goes to Antarctica and comes back a changed person — and the science is the reason.

title: My Daddy Is a Cowboy
author: Stephanie Seales
heads_up: AUTHOR/ILLUSTRATOR NAMES NEED VERIFICATION BEFORE SUPABASE IMPORT.
orbital_description: The Caldecott Honor and Coretta Scott King Illustrator Award winner opens the geography of an American city at dawn, the history of Black cowboys as an erased part of American Western history, and the social-emotional architecture of father-daughter time as a deliberate ritual. Six starred reviews.
hook: A girl and her father take an early morning horseback ride through their city — six starred reviews for a book about just-us time that the Coretta Scott King committee found essential.

title: My Father's Dragon
author: Ruth Stiles Gannett
orbital_description: The book is a problem-solving masterclass: each animal encounter requires a different solution using only what Elmer packed. Children naturally start thinking about what they would pack and why. The animals' logic is perfectly consistent.
hook: A boy rescues a baby dragon from Wild Island using a knapsack full of ordinary objects — and the packing list is half the lesson.

title: My Heart Fills with Happiness
author: Monique Gray Smith
orbital_description: Sensory experience as emotional vocabulary. Cree and Metis visual culture. Board book.
hook: Bannock baking with a grandmother. Drumming and singing. These are the moments when a heart fills up.

title: My Presentation Today Is About the Anaconda
author: Bibi Dumon Tak
orbital_description: Kirkus Spring 2025 Preview praised as 'raises the bar for animal nonfiction.' Translated from Dutch. Every animal's oral report is delivered from its ecological relationship to the subject, making the science accurate and the humor arise from perspective. The format teaches predator-prey relationships, habitat ecology, and the scientific names of real species.
hook: A cleaner fish gives a presentation about sharks. A white rhino presents on the shoebill stork. An earthworm delivers the definitive report on anacondas. Animal class reports — scientifically accurate and completely hilarious.

title: My Side of the Mountain
author: Jean Craighead George
orbital_description: The book opens Catskill ecology, raptor training, edible plant identification, shelter building, and the art of scientific observation through a first-person journal format. Sam's decisions are based on real woodcraft. After this one, children look at trees differently.
hook: A twelve-year-old runs away to the Catskill Mountains and survives an entire year alone — and every technique is real and accurate.

title: My Strange Shrinking Parents
author: Zeno Sworder
orbital_description: The book opens immigration history, the economics of labor and sacrifice, and the specific experience of watching parents diminish from overwork while a child grows. Sworder's visual metaphor makes abstract economic struggle tangible for children who may be living versions of this story.
hook: A child's parents immigrate and work so hard they seem to physically shrink — and the visual metaphor is one of the most honest things picture books have ever done.

title: Napesni/Renegade: The Story of a Wild Bison
author: Marcie R. Rendon
orbital_description: Fall 2025. Marcie R. Rendon (Cash and the Bears, multiple award-winning Ojibwe author) opens the history of American bison from 30 million animals to near extinction in the 1880s through the specific story of one wild bison's capture and relocation, the ecology of bison as keystone species in prairie ecosystems, and the indigenous land rights context of bison management on reservations.
hook: Napesni is a wild bison — captured on the Cheyenne River Reservation and relocated to Minnesota, in the latest chapter of a 200-year story of bison nearly going extinct and slowly coming back.

title: Neighborhood Sharks
author: Katherine Roy
heads_up: Predation described in detail.
orbital_description: Anatomically precise illustrations. Shark physiology, elephant seal ecology, field observation methodology.
hook: Thirty miles from San Francisco, great white sharks hunt elephant seals every autumn. Sibert Medal.

title: Nell Plants a Tree
author: Anne Wynter
orbital_description: The book opens forest ecology through a tree's life cycle, community history through the generations of people who grow up near the same tree, and the philosophy of planting something you won't live to see fully grown. Miyares's illustrations render the passage of time through changing light and season.
hook: Nell plants a tree — and the book follows that tree across 100 years of community life.

title: Neon
author: Jason Reynolds
orbital_description: The Coretta Scott King Author Award winner with seven starred reviews opens the social-emotional architecture of Black girl joy — not as a coping mechanism but as a genuine state of being — community and neighborhood as identity anchors, music as a language of belonging, and friendship as the specific infrastructure that makes all of it possible.
hook: Aria is Neon — and the book gives readers a girl who is luminous in her own right, without asking anyone's permission.

title: Never Caught, the Story of Ona Judge
author: Erica Armstrong Dunbar and Kathleen Van Cleve
orbital_description: Opens the specific history of enslaved people in George Washington's household, the legal structure of the Fugitive Slave Act through the mechanism Washington used to try to recapture her, the geography of Ona's escape route from Philadelphia to Portsmouth, New Hampshire, and the concept of George Washington as a historical figure whose contradictions are essential to understand.
hook: Ona Judge was enslaved in George Washington's household — and on a May evening in 1796, she walked out the front door and never came back. Washington spent years trying to recapture her.

title: Never Give Up: Dr. Katalin Karikó and the Race to the COVID-19 Vaccine
author: Debbie Dadey
orbital_description: The book opens mRNA biology and how vaccines train the immune system, the history of the COVID-19 pandemic, the social science of institutional rejection and scientific persistence, and the concept of Nobel Prize-level work emerging from decades of ignored research.
hook: Dr. Katalin Karikó was told for 40 years that mRNA vaccines were a dead end — and she kept working anyway.

title: Night Chef
author: Mika Song
orbital_description: 2025 graphic novel from the creator of the Norma and Belly series. Opens culinary arts and the culture of French restaurant kitchens, the concept of cooking as a craft that rewards observation and improvisation, and the social-emotional story of an outsider who has more to offer than the establishment is prepared to accept.
hook: A raccoon who secretly lives inside a fine French restaurant has one dream: becoming a chef. And his path is entirely his own.

title: Night Walk
author: Jason Cockcroft
heads_up: Touches on loss and grief; discuss before reading.
orbital_description: Confirmed February 18, 2025. Opens the astronomy of the night sky through the specific stars Grandma loved, the geography of a quiet nighttime walk, and the social-emotional concept of grief carried gently through a grandparent-grandchild relationship. The night sky becomes a place where someone is kept.
hook: A boy and his grandfather walk to Grandma's favorite place at night — and the walk is about loss and what the stars keep.

title: Nightsong
author: Sally Soweol Han
orbital_description: SLJ Best Picture Book 2025. Opens the physics of sound through the acoustic experience of a city at night, the concept of active listening as a scientifically distinct cognitive state, and the natural sources of sound that persist inside urban environments. Han's illustrations have the quality of watercolors brushed across clouds.
hook: A boy rides home on a bus and discovers the whole world is singing to him — the rain, the wind, the city — if he's still and listening.

title: NOA
author: Aaron Becker
orbital_description: The wordless 2024 picture book from the Caldecott Honor illustrator of the Journey trilogy opens climate science through the visual evidence of a flooded future, robotics through the mechanics of a caretaking machine, and conservation biology through the specific animals NOA collects and protects. Children narrate the story themselves from Becker's meticulously rendered images.
hook: A robot becomes the guardian of the last animals in a flooded world — and builds an ark with nothing but care and precision.

title: Not a Box
author: Antoinette Portis
orbital_description: The simplest lesson in creative thinking. The foundational cognitive move behind engineering, art, and science.
hook: It is not a box. It is a race car. It is not a box. It is a mountain. Except it is definitely a box.

title: Octopus Moon
author: Bobbie Pyron
orbital_description: 2026 Schneider Family Book Award Honor (middle grade). Opens octopus neurobiology including distributed intelligence across eight arms, camouflage and color-change mechanisms, and the cognitive science of octopus problem-solving, alongside the social-emotional experience of a child who finds connection through an animal that also lives in its own particular way. NOTE: Likely pub 2025 — verify before placing on 2026 shelf.
hook: A child obsessed with octopuses finds in them a mirror for her own way of being in the world — and the marine biology is as rich as the emotional story.

title: Octopus Ocean: Geniuses of the Deep
author: Mark Leiren-Young
orbital_description: SLJ 2025 Stars So Far starred review. Opens octopus neurobiology including the distributed nervous system with autonomous arms, the chromatophores that produce camouflage faster than any computer, the cognitive flexibility demonstrated in laboratory problem-solving, and the evolutionary puzzle of how an invertebrate became so intelligent.
hook: Octopuses have three hearts, blue blood, and nine brains — one central and one in each arm. And their intelligence, once controversial, is now accepted as one of the great puzzles of animal cognition.

title: Ode to Grapefruit: How James Earl Jones Found His Voice
author: Kari Lavelle
heads_up: PUB YEAR NEEDS VERIFICATION. Orbis Pictus Honor 2025 = likely 2024 pub. Verify before placing in 2025 batch.
orbital_description: Orbis Pictus Honor 2025 (announced November 2024 NCTE convention, likely for books published in 2024). NOTE: PUB YEAR MAY BE 2024 — verify before placing in 2025 batch. Bryan Collier (multiple Caldecott Honor illustrator) opens the neuroscience of stuttering through James Earl Jones's biography, the therapeutic use of poetry and performance as speech tools, and the concept that a voice found through difficulty carries more power for what it overcame.
hook: James Earl Jones grew up with a stutter so severe he didn't speak aloud for years — and poetry gave him back his voice.

title: Officer Buckle and Gloria
author: Peggy Rathmann
orbital_description: The book teaches real safety rules, partnership and trust, the difference between knowing something and making it visible, and the collapse when trust breaks. The reconciliation models healthy repair of a damaged relationship.
hook: Officer Buckle's safety speeches put everyone to sleep — until Gloria the police dog starts acting them out behind his back.

title: Old Blue Is My Home
author: Lita Judge
orbital_description: SLJ Best Picture Book 2025. Based on the author's own childhood, the book opens the social-emotional experience of housing insecurity from a child's perspective, the geography of a neighborhood seen from a van, and the concept of home as a feeling rather than a structure. The illustration technique of iridescent watercolor and colored pencil gives the van a warmth that matches the family's.
hook: A child's home is an old blue van — and the story of wanting to belong to a more conventional world is told with iridescent watercolor and absolute love.

title: Omnivore's Dilemma: Young Readers Edition
author: Michael Pollan
orbital_description: Adapted from Pollan's landmark adult work for young readers. Opens the ecology and economics of the American industrial food system through corn as its foundational crop, the concept of a food chain as a linked system from soil to stomach, the chemistry of photosynthesis as the origin of all food calories, and the comparison of industrial vs. local vs. hunter-gatherer food systems.
hook: Where does your food actually come from — and what does the answer reveal about modern agriculture, ecology, and what it means to eat?

title: Once Upon a Book
author: Grace Lin
orbital_description: The book opens literary self-reference, the act of reading as transformation, and the history of storytelling through Lin's richly illustrated exploration of a girl who enters her own book. The recursive structure teaches narrative meta-cognition before children have a word for it.
hook: A girl opens a book and falls directly into it — and the book inside the book is this one.

title: One Day a Mayfly
author: Shirley Marr
heads_up: Contains depiction of death as a natural conclusion; discuss as needed.
orbital_description: Confirmed May 27, 2025. Opens the entomology of Ephemeroptera (mayflies) including the extraordinary biology of an insect that lives its adult life in a single day after spending up to two years as a nymph, the urban freshwater ecology of a city lake, and the philosophical concept of a life measured in quality rather than duration.
hook: A mayfly has exactly one day to live — and she seizes it completely, flying over a lake and the city around it.

title: One Day at the Bottom of the Deep Blue Sea
author: Daniel Bernstrom
orbital_description: Confirmed April 29, 2025. Daniel Bernstrom (One Day in the Eucalyptus, Eucalyptus Tree) opens deep-sea biology through the adventure structure of a girl navigating the ocean's darkest layers, the ecology of deep-sea predators, and the specific creatures that live below the light zone.
hook: A girl outsmarts a hungry shark in the deep sea — and the book is both a rollicking adventure and a precise inventory of the deep ocean's extraordinary creatures.

title: One Grain of Rice
author: Demi
orbital_description: Exponential doubling taught through an Indian folktale with gold-leaf illustrations.
hook: A clever girl asks a greedy raja for one grain of rice, doubled each day for thirty days.

title: Our Lake
author: Angie Kang
orbital_description: The 2026 Caldecott Honor Book opens freshwater lake ecology across seasons, the role of shared natural spaces in community formation, and the concept of belonging rooted in place. Kang's illustration style captures both the science of seasonal lake change and the human warmth of a community organized around a shared resource.
hook: A community gathers around a lake through every season — and the lake holds them all.

title: Our Mothers' Names: Love in Many Languages
author: Uma Menon
orbital_description: Confirmed April 22, 2025. Opens the linguistics of maternal naming conventions across languages — Amma, Ima, Māmā, Máthair — and the concept that language is a container for culture, so that what you call your mother tells the story of who you are.
hook: Children all over the world call their mothers something different — and the language they use holds the whole culture it comes from.

title: Out of My Mind
author: Sharon M. Draper
heads_up: Depicts ableism, social exclusion, and a deeply unfair moment near the end that is not resolved with a tidy victory. Emotionally challenging.
orbital_description: Sharon Draper writes from inside Melody's mind: a girl with a photographic memory and cerebral palsy who cannot walk, talk, or feed herself. The world assumes she is intellectually disabled. She is not. When she gets a communication device, everything changes, and the school's response to her intelligence reveals more about ableism than any textbook could. The child absorbs neurology (how cerebral palsy affects motor function but not cognition), assistive technology, the social construction of intelligence, and the question of what happens when the assumptions people make about you are shattered.
hook: Melody has cerebral palsy. She cannot speak. She is the smartest kid in the school, and nobody knows.

title: Over and Under the Pond
author: Kate Messner
orbital_description: Splits the world in half. Above: a quiet canoe ride. Below: an entire ecosystem.
hook: A mother and son paddle across a pond while an entire hidden world hunts and builds beneath their boat.

title: Over in the Meadow
author: John Langstaff
orbital_description: Each verse introduces a real animal in its real habitat doing a real behavior: turtles, frogs, bluebirds, squirrels. The counting structure layers math onto ecology, and the rhyme makes both stick.
hook: A counting song about meadow animals and their young — ten mother-child pairs, ten different behaviors, one meadow ecosystem.

title: Owl and Penguin
author: Vikram Madan
orbital_description: The 2023 Geisel Honor book opens owl and penguin biology through the friendship structure: owls are nocturnal hunters, penguins are diurnal social birds, and their radically different schedules are accurate science. The humor arises from the real ecological conflict.
hook: Owl loves night and Penguin loves day — and their schedule conflict is the funniest biology lesson in early reader history.

title: Owl at Home
author: Arnold Lobel
orbital_description: The stories blend owl behavior, night ecology, and winter seasons with philosophy about loneliness, friendship, and the nature of comfort. Owl's attempts to understand natural phenomena model scientific curiosity at its most charming.
hook: Owl tries to befriend the moon, worries about bumps in the night, and makes tear-water tea — quiet, strange, and deeply comforting.

title: Owl Moon
author: Jane Yolen
orbital_description: Owl Moon is a masterclass in nature observation: types of owls, nocturnal behavior, winter ecology, and the art of stillness. The parent-child relationship models scientific patience. After this one a child will listen differently outdoors.
hook: A child and her father hike into the snowy woods at night to find an owl — and the silence teaches more than any classroom could.

title: Ox-Cart Man
author: Donald Hall
orbital_description: The entire annual economic cycle of a 19th-century farm family. Caldecott Medal.
hook: A New England farmer sells everything his family made all year, including the cart and the ox, and walks home to start over.

title: Papá's Magical Water-Jug Clock
author: Jesús Trejo
orbital_description: The 2024 Belpré Honor book opens the history of water clocks as one of the first timekeeping technologies, the physics of water flow, and the economics of a family that makes its own tools. The clock is real and buildable; the science is accurate.
hook: A boy's father builds a clock out of water jugs — and the invention teaches both time measurement and the engineering of improvisation.

title: Pencil
author: Hye-Eun Kim
orbital_description: NYT / NYPL Best Illustrated Children's Book 2025. Opens the philosophy of creative process through the paradox of a pencil drawing the hand that holds it, the concept of line as the foundation of visual art, and the social-emotional idea that making something transforms the maker.
hook: A pencil and a child draw each other into being — and the concept of imagination as a creative loop opens the very nature of drawing.

title: Phoenix
author: Kimberly Brubaker Bradley
orbital_description: Pub March 3, 2026.
