Kimberly Brubaker Bradley (Jefferson's Sons, Fighting Words, Newbery Honor) opens equine behavior and communication science, the rural geography and economics of horse country, and the SEL architecture of a trust relationship built between a child and an animal that has learned not to trust humans.
hook: A horse named Phoenix and the girl who refuses to give up on him — the first book in a new series from the Newbery Honor-winning author of Fighting Words.

title: Plague-Busters! A Germ-Hunter's History of Fighting Deadly Disease
author: Lindsey Fitzharris
heads_up: Describes historical medical practices and death from disease; preview for appropriateness.
orbital_description: The book opens epidemiology through the history of plague, smallpox, cholera, and rabies, the development of germ theory as a scientific paradigm shift, the specific courage of doctors who experimented on themselves, and the social conditions that allowed disease to spread. The history of medicine is the history of scientific method under pressure.
hook: Every disease that has killed millions of people was once a complete mystery — and this book shows the doctors who solved them, including the ones who died trying.

title: Planting a Rainbow
author: Lois Ehlert
orbital_description: The book introduces botanical vocabulary including bulbs, seedlings, and petals alongside color theory and seasonal plant cycles. Every flower is identifiable, making this simultaneously a field guide and a color lesson.
hook: A family plants bulbs in fall and watches every color of the rainbow bloom in spring — and every flower named is real.

title: Planting the Wild Garden
author: Kathryn O. Galbraith
orbital_description: Kathryn Galbraith follows seeds as they travel by wind, water, animal fur, bird droppings, and human shoes. Wendy Anderson Halperin's delicate pencil-and-watercolor illustrations carry botanical accuracy (seed morphology, dispersal mechanisms, germination conditions) while the text carries the poetry of unintentional planting. The child absorbs plant biology, seed engineering, ecosystem interdependence, and the idea that wildflowers are the product of systems, not accidents.
hook: Who plants the flowers that nobody planted? The wind, the river, the birds, the bears, and you.

title: Please Pay Attention
author: Jamie Sumner
heads_up: School shooting is the central trauma; discuss before reading. Includes recovery from PTSD.
orbital_description: The ALSC 2026 Notable Book opens post-traumatic stress through the neuroscience of trauma response, equine therapy science including how horses read human nervous systems, and the experience of navigating both disability and PTSD simultaneously. The verse format models how poetry compresses unbearable experience into something survivable.
hook: Bea survived a school shooting and uses a wheelchair — and the horse therapy center is the first place that treats neither fact as the whole of who she is.

title: Pocket
author: Katherine Applegate
heads_up: Depicts the realities of war; discuss before reading.
orbital_description: The book opens WWI history through the specific experience of what soldiers carried, the psychology of comfort objects, and the arc of a toy's life across a century. Applegate brings her signature tenderness for the perspective of an overlooked narrator to a genuinely historical framing.
hook: A toy bear made to ride in a soldier's pocket through WWI has outlasted everyone he was made to comfort — and is still trying to be useful.

title: Pony
author: R.J. Palacio
heads_up: Violence depicted in a frontier context.
orbital_description: The book opens 1860s Ohio and frontier geography, the natural history of the American frontier including landscapes and wildlife, and the ethics of frontier justice. Palacio layers supernatural elements over historically accurate terrain.
hook: A boy follows strangers into the frontier wilderness to rescue his father — and a ghostly white pony shows him the way.

title: Popcorn
author: Rob Harrell
heads_up: PUB YEAR NEEDS VERIFICATION — Charlotte Huck Honor 2025 = likely 2024 pub. Verify before placing in 2025 batch.
orbital_description: Charlotte Huck Honor 2025 (for 2024 pub). NOTE: Likely a 2024 pub honored in November 2024. Verification required before placing in 2025 batch. Rob Harrell (Wink, Batpig) opens the animal cognition of dogs through fiction — the specific logic of a dog's experience of time, smell, and relationship — and the social-emotional concept of unconditional attachment.
hook: A family dog named Popcorn narrates his life — and his version of reality is so joyfully, absolutely, precisely dog-brained that it changes how you see every moment.

title: Popo the Xolo
author: Paloma Angelina Lopez
orbital_description: The 2026 Belpré Illustrator Award winner opens the biology and history of the xoloitzcuintli (Mexican hairless dog), the Aztec mythology in which xolos guided souls through the underworld, the geography of pre-Columbian Mexico, and the concept of ancient breeds as living history. Matias's art earned the Belpré Illustrator Award.
hook: The xoloitzcuintli is the oldest dog breed in the Americas — and this is the story of one who has been a companion to people across 3,000 years of Mexican history.

title: Poppy
author: Avi
orbital_description: The owl's actual hunting behaviors including silent flight and head rotation are accurate; the new corn field represents real agricultural land use; the porcupine quill as weapon is real physics. The heroism is built entirely on biology.
hook: A mouse named Poppy defeats a great horned owl with nothing but courage and a porcupine quill — and the defeat is logically, ornithologically honest.

title: Port Chicago 50: Disaster, Mutiny, and the Fight for Civil Rights
author: Steve Sheinkin
orbital_description: National Book Award finalist. Opens the history of segregation in the US military through the specific conditions at Port Chicago, the chemistry and physics of the 1944 explosion that killed more than 320 men, the legal history of the mutiny trial and its civil rights implications, and the geography of the San Francisco Bay as a WWII naval logistics center.
hook: 50 Black Navy sailors refused dangerous orders after an explosion killed 320 of their servicemen — and were charged with mutiny, launching one of the most important civil rights cases of WWII.

title: Press Here
author: Herve Tullet
orbital_description: Tullet instructs the child to press, shake, tilt, and blow on the pages. Input produces output. The child IS the processor.
hook: Press the yellow dot. Turn the page. You did that. The first interactive book that uses no technology.

title: Radiant
author: Vaunda Micheaux Nelson
orbital_description: ALSC 2026 Notable. Vaunda Micheaux Nelson (No Crystal Stair, Coretta Scott King Author Honor) opens the civil rights era in the North through the specific geography of an integrated but hostile school in 1963 Pennsylvania, the economics of domestic work, and the social-emotional experience of being the only Black child in a white classroom at the moment civil rights law was being made.
hook: It is 1963 in a small Pennsylvania town, and Cooper Dale is the only Black student in the class — and aches to shine like her mama, a house cleaner for the bully's wealthy white family.

title: Radium Girls (Young Readers Edition)
author: Kate Moore
heads_up: Contains descriptions of severe illness.
orbital_description: Opens the chemistry and biology of radiation through radium's specific effects on bones and tissue (radium deposits in the skeleton by mimicking calcium), the history of the labor rights movement through the legal battle these women waged against a corporation that knew the dangers, and the history of occupational health standards as a consequence of this specific case.
hook: Young women who painted watch dials with radium paint in the 1920s were told to lick their brushes — and the science of what happened next changed labor law in America forever.

title: Rain!
author: Linda Ashman
orbital_description: A grumpy man and a joyful boy experience the same rainstorm from opposite emotional poles. Christian Robinson's collage illustrations carry the meteorology (clouds forming, puddles collecting) while the text carries the emotional science: how the same event lands differently depending on who you are. The book resolves with connection, not correction. Neither reaction is wrong.
hook: Same rain, two people, completely different days. The simplest lesson in perspective your child will ever get.

title: Ramona Quimby Age 8
author: Beverly Cleary
orbital_description: The book opens family economic stress, the social dynamics of third grade, and the experience of not-quite-fitting-in. Ramona's observation that everyone is acting all the time is a genuine social insight children keep for life.
hook: Third-grade Ramona navigates the humiliation of throwing up at school, her father's job loss, and a raw egg in her hair — and grows up without noticing.

title: Ramona the Pest
author: Beverly Cleary
orbital_description: Ramona opens school as a social and academic system, the experience of being misunderstood, Portland Oregon as a real place, and the gap between what children intend and what adults observe. Ramona is always right about her intentions.
hook: Ramona starts kindergarten and immediately becomes the most complicated child in the class — through no fault of her own.

title: Ranger's Apprentice: The Ruins of Gorlan
author: John Flanagan
orbital_description: Opens the history of medieval intelligence operations, archery as a physics problem (trajectory, draw weight, wind compensation), wilderness tracking as a science of observation and inference, and the feudal social structure through the ward system that defines Will's status.
hook: Will is chosen as apprentice to a Ranger — the king's secret corps of trackers and archers — and discovers that being small and overlooked is the greatest advantage a spy can have.

title: Reaching for the Moon: The Autobiography of NASA Mathematician Katherine Johnson
author: Katherine Johnson
orbital_description: Newbery Honor 2020. Opens orbital mechanics through Johnson's own explanation of the mathematics she performed, the history of segregation at NASA through her specific experience, the geography of West Virginia through her childhood landscape, and the concept of a life in mathematics as both beauty and utility.
hook: Katherine Johnson's own account — in her own words — of growing up in segregated West Virginia, falling in love with math, and calculating the trajectory that sent humans to the moon.

title: Redwall
author: Brian Jacques
heads_up: Contains battle violence appropriate to the medieval setting.
orbital_description: Opens the history of medieval siege warfare through the tactical structures of the battle for Redwall, the ecology of woodland animals through the abbey community's social organization, the concept of heroism as discovered rather than inherited, and the culinary tradition of English abbeys through extraordinary food descriptions at every meal.
hook: A young mouse must find the legendary sword of the great Martin the Warrior to defend Redwall Abbey from a rat warlord — and he is nobody's idea of a hero.

title: Remarkably Ruby
author: Terri Libenson
orbital_description: The graphic novel opens dual narrative perspective as a formal technique, the social dynamics of the transition from elementary to middle school, anxiety as a hidden experience invisible from the outside, and theater as a space for identity exploration. The hybrid text-and-comics format models two reading modes simultaneously.
hook: Two fifth-grade girls tell the same story from radically different perspectives — and neither of them sees the whole picture.

title: Revolutionary Mary: The True Story of One Woman, the Declaration of Independence, and America's Fight for Freedom
author: Karen Blumenthal and Jen McCartney
orbital_description: The multiple starred-review picture book biography opens the history of the printing press as a tool of revolution, the specific history of American newspapers during the Revolutionary War, the biography of Mary Katharine Goddard as publisher and postmaster, and the deliberate erasure of women's contributions from the official historical record.
hook: Mary Katharine Goddard was the only woman whose name was printed on the Declaration of Independence — and almost nobody knows it.

title: Rising Water: The Story of the Thai Cave Rescue
author: Marc Aronson
heads_up: Contains descriptions of a rescue diver's death.
orbital_description: Opens the geology of karst cave systems through the specific structure of the Tham Luang cave network, the physics of cave diving through oxygen and nitrogen management required for rescue, the engineering of the rescue solution (sedating children and carrying them through flooded passages), and the international coordination of experts from 17 countries.
hook: 12 boys and their soccer coach trapped in a flooding cave in Thailand in 2018 — and the international rescue operation that required inventing a solution that had never been attempted.

title: Rosie Revere, Engineer
author: Andrea Beaty
orbital_description: Engineering meets WWII history meets growth mindset. The design process is embedded in the plot.
hook: A quiet girl who builds inventions in secret learns from her great-great-aunt that the only real failure is quitting.

title: Roxaboxen
author: Alice McLerran
orbital_description: Roxaboxen is a meditation on imaginative world-building, the geography of childhood, and how play creates genuine social structures with rules, roles, and consequences. The desert southwest setting anchors the imagination in a real landscape children can find on a map.
hook: Children in an Arizona town build a city from stones and bottles in the desert — and it becomes more real than the real town.

title: Rumpelstiltskin
author: Mac Barnett
orbital_description: Kirkus Spring 2026 Preview top pick, starred review. Opens the European folk tale tradition of bargain stories, the history of spinning and weaving as women's economic labor, the geography of medieval German culture embedded in the original tale, and the craft of fairy tale retelling as literary art. Ellis's art earned the comparison to medieval manuscript illumination.
hook: The classic fairy tale retold by Mac Barnett with his trademark sly wit — and Carson Ellis's illustrations evoking medieval tapestries make it genuinely feel like something pulled from centuries ago.

title: Runaway Bunny
author: Margaret Wise Brown
orbital_description: The book is an unconditional love guarantee in picture form. Each escape opens a different science domain: clouds, winds, rivers, mountains, the sea. The rabbit's mother transforms to match each environment, modeling a beautiful lesson in adaptation.
hook: A little bunny runs away in every possible form — and his mother follows through mountains and rivers and air until he gives up and stays.

title: Ruthie Rose's Big Idea: A Poetry Story
author: John Schu
orbital_description: Confirmed March 4, 2025. John Schu (librarian and children's literature ambassador) opens the literary form of poetry as a way of capturing and sharing ideas, the social-emotional experience of creative vulnerability, and the concept that ideas multiply rather than diminish when shared.
hook: A girl has a big idea she's afraid to share — and she discovers that sharing is how ideas grow.

title: Sakina and the Uninvited Guests
author: Zahra Marwan
orbital_description: Confirmed April 29, 2025. Zahra Marwan opens the concept of art as a time machine — how paintings and sculptures contain the lives of people who made and used them — the history embedded in museum collections, and the social-emotional concept of finding yourself reflected in historical art.
hook: A girl visits a museum with her mother and discovers that art connects her to history and to herself in ways she didn't expect — including some 'uninvited guests' from the past.

title: Sam and Dave Dig a Hole
author: Mac Barnett
orbital_description: The book is secretly a geology lesson: underground layers, diamond formation, and what lies beneath the surface. The irony of the boys just missing the gem is hilarious; the ending invites children to figure out what really happened.
hook: Sam and Dave dig straight down looking for something spectacular — and pass right by diamonds without knowing it.

title: Santiago Saw Things Differently: Santiago Ramón y Cajal, Scientific Artist
author: Christine Iverson
orbital_description: The book opens the history of neuroscience through the career of Santiago Ramón y Cajal, who won the 1906 Nobel Prize for proving that the brain is made of individual cells rather than a continuous network. His detailed drawings of neurons are some of the most beautiful scientific illustrations ever made, and they were made because he loved art.
hook: A boy who loved to draw got in trouble for his drawings his whole life — until he started drawing neurons, and the drawings changed how humans understand the brain.

title: Sashimi: A Graphic Novel
author: Dan Santat
orbital_description: Multiple starred reviews (Booklist, PW). From the Caldecott Medal-winning illustrator of The Adventures of Beekle. Opens the biology of aquatic life through the lens of a character who lives between two worlds, the social-emotional experience of never fully belonging to any group, and the graphic novel technique of using visual gags to carry emotional weight. Series opener. Pub April 14, 2026.
hook: Sashimi has the body of a boy and the head of a fish, and his first day at elementary school is both a mystery and a catastrophe — and he is trying to find a sea monster and also himself.

title: Savvy
author: Ingrid Law
orbital_description: The Kansas landscape is real and specific; each savvy is a metaphor for adolescent identity; the bus journey maps US highway geography. The book celebrates unexpected family configurations and the courage of not knowing your own strength.
hook: On Mibs Beaumont's thirteenth birthday she gets a savvy — a magical power — and sets off across Kansas on a bus to save her father.

title: Sea in My Cells
author: Laura Alary
orbital_description: Confirmed May 6, 2025. Opens the biology of water in living cells including the water content of blood, tears, and cytoplasm, the evolutionary history of life emerging from the ocean, and the water cycle as the loop that connects the sea to every living body. After this book, children understand why they are made of water.
hook: Water travels from the sea to your glass to your cells — and the journey is a story of how life on Earth began and continues.

title: Seeds of Change
author: Jen Cullerton Johnson
heads_up: References political violence against Wangari. Age-appropriate.
orbital_description: Jen Cullerton Johnson tells the Wangari Maathai story with emphasis on the scientific training: Wangari earned a biology degree, studied soil science, and applied empirical methods to understand why Kenya's forests were disappearing. This version carries the academic rigor behind the activism, which distinguishes it from the other Wangari books in the Library. Sonia Lynn Sadler's batik-inspired illustrations carry East African visual traditions and botanical precision. The child absorbs the link between education and environmental action, soil science, deforestation ecology, and the fact that Wangari's activism was data-driven before it was emotional.
hook: Wangari Maathai studied biology in America. She brought the science home to Kenya. The trees were the proof.

title: Seeds of Discovery: How Barbara McClintock Used Corn and Curiosity to Solve a Science Mystery and Win a Nobel Prize
author: Lori Alexander
orbital_description: SLJ 2025 Stars So Far starred review. Opens genetics through the specific mechanism of transposons (jumping genes) that McClintock discovered through careful visual observation of corn kernel patterns, the history of genetics from Mendel to the molecular biology era, and the institutional story of a woman working alone because mainstream science refused to take her seriously until she won the Nobel.
hook: Barbara McClintock spent 30 years studying the genetics of corn plants — and was laughed at for decades before her discovery of jumping genes won the Nobel Prize.

title: Seen and Unseen: What Dorothea Lange, Toyo Miyatake, and Ansel Adams's Photographs Reveal About the Japanese American Incarceration
author: Elizabeth Partridge
orbital_description: The 2023 Sibert Medal winner opens WWII domestic history through the specific injustice of Japanese American incarceration, photography as documentary evidence and propaganda simultaneously, and the visual literacy required to read an image critically. Tamaki's illustrations of the three photographers' distinct styles model exactly what the text describes.
hook: Three photographers documented the same incarceration camps — and their photographs reveal how the same truth looks completely different depending on who is holding the camera.

title: Separate Is Never Equal
author: Duncan Tonatiuh
heads_up: Racial discrimination described directly.
orbital_description: Mendez v. Westminster is the civil rights case most Americans have never heard of.
hook: Seven years before Brown v. Board, a Mexican-American family in California sued to desegregate their school, and won.

title: Separate Is Never Equal
author: Duncan Tonatiuh
heads_up: Depicts racial segregation and discrimination. Age-appropriate, handled with dignity.
orbital_description: Duncan Tonatiuh tells the story of Mendez v. Westminster (1947) through the eyes of Sylvia Mendez, who was turned away from a whites-only school in Orange County. Tonatiuh's Mixtec-inspired art carries the legal architecture (courtroom scenes, legal arguments, appellate structure) alongside 1940s California agriculture and Mexican-American community life. The child absorbs civil rights legal history, the relationship between Mendez and Brown v. Board, the California citrus economy, and the mechanics of how a lawsuit becomes a precedent. Most children have never heard of this case. They should.
hook: Seven years before Brown v. Board of Education, a Mexican-American family sued to desegregate California schools. They won.

title: Seven Skies All at Once
author: Ted Kooser
orbital_description: Confirmed July 8, 2025. Ted Kooser (former U.S. Poet Laureate) opens the meteorology of a storm system through the sensory experience of watching a sky change — the specific color shifts of cumulonimbus clouds, the stillness before lightning, the smell of rain — using poetry as the precise instrument for weather observation.
hook: Seven poems for seven different skies — and the art captures every mood of the sky before, during, and after a rainstorm.

title: Shark Lady
author: Jess Keating
orbital_description: Japanese-American, female, studying sharks in the 1950s. The scientific establishment dismissed her three times over.
hook: Everyone said sharks were mindless killers. Eugenie Clark spent her life proving everyone wrong.

title: Shining Star: Vera Rubin Discovers Dark Matter
author: Suzanne Slade
orbital_description: The book opens astrophysics through the concept of dark matter, the methods of observational astronomy, the history of women's exclusion from professional science, and the specific courage of continuing research that institutional consensus dismissed. Dark matter now accounts for 27% of the observable universe.
hook: Vera Rubin discovered that most of the universe is invisible — and she was ignored for years before anyone believed her.

title: Shipwreck at the Bottom of the World
author: Jennifer Armstrong
heads_up: Extreme survival. Animal killing for food.
orbital_description: The greatest survival story ever documented, with period photographs.
hook: Shackleton's ship was crushed by Antarctic ice. He kept 27 men alive for two years on floating ice.

title: Shipwrecked! by the Laws of Science: How 14 Shipwrecks Redefined What We Know About Our World
author: Martin W. Sandler
orbital_description: The 2024 Sibert Honor book opens marine archaeology as a scientific discipline, the geology of the ocean floor, the chemistry of wreck preservation, and the history embedded in specific shipwrecks from the 14th-century Chinese merchant ship Shinan to the polar exploration ships HMS Erebus and Terror. Each wreck opens a different history.
hook: Every sunken ship is a time capsule — and fourteen of them redefined what we know about the ancient world, navigation, and the ocean floor.

title: Sideways Stories from Wayside School
author: Louis Sachar
orbital_description: The book models narrative structure through chapters that are each a complete story with internal logic. It celebrates the weirdness of every person in every community and teaches children that fiction's rules can be broken with intention.
hook: Wayside School was built sideways by accident — thirty floors, one classroom each — and every story is its own perfectly calibrated absurdity.

title: Simon Sort of Says
author: Erin Bow
orbital_description: The book opens radio frequency science through the National Radio Quiet Zone in Green Bank, West Virginia — a real place where cell phones and internet are banned to protect radio telescope research. The comic tone masks a genuinely serious examination of privacy, internet permanence, and the right to be forgotten.
hook: Simon's family moves to a town with no internet so he can escape the viral video that destroyed his life — and the silence turns out to be its own kind of loud.

title: Six Dots: A Story of Young Louis Braille
author: Jen Bryant
heads_up: Depicts the childhood accident that caused Braille's blindness. Brief, not graphic, but the awl-to-the-eye detail is present.
orbital_description: Jen Bryant tells Louis Braille's story from the accident that blinded him at three through his enrollment at the Royal Institute and his invention of the six-dot code. Boris Kulikov's illustrations carry 19th-century French architecture, the mechanics of embossing, and the military night-writing system Braille adapted. The child absorbs biography, French history, coding and encryption concepts, disability as innovation driver, and the tactile physics of reading. The invention did not succeed in his lifetime. It succeeded after.
hook: A blind boy in 19th-century France invented a language you can read with your fingers. He was fifteen.

title: Spy School
author: Stuart Gibbs
orbital_description: Opens the geography of Washington DC through the covert locations where the CIA's fictional academy hides, the history of American intelligence through the missions Ben stumbles through, and the comic social dynamics of a child pretending to be a trained spy. Humor is precise and pacing is fast. Gateway for reluctant readers.
hook: Ben Ripley is recruited to a secret CIA academy as bait to catch a dangerous enemy agent — and he has absolutely no idea what he's doing, which turns out to be his greatest asset.

title: Stalactite and Stalagmite: A Big Tale From a Little Cave
author: Drew Beckmeyer
orbital_description: The 2026 Caldecott Honor Book opens the geology of cave formation including stalactite and stalagmite chemistry and growth rates, deep time in geological terms, and evolutionary history from dinosaur extinction through the present. The comedic frame of two small rocks arguing about what they've witnessed over 65 million years is perfectly calibrated.
hook: Two tiny cave formations witness the entire history of the world — from the dinosaurs to right now — and can't quite agree on what it means.

title: Stamped: Racism, Antiracism, and You
author: Jason Reynolds & Ibram X. Kendi
heads_up: Discusses slavery, violence, and systemic oppression throughout. Jason Reynolds handles it with care but the content is heavy. Best for mature 8-10 readers or 10-12.
orbital_description: Jason Reynolds remixes Ibram X. Kendi's Stamped from the Beginning for young readers. The book traces racist ideas from 15th-century Europe through American history, naming the intellectuals, politicians, and systems that created and maintained them. Reynolds writes with his signature directness and humor, and the structure teaches historiography: not what happened, but why the story was told that way. The child absorbs five centuries of intellectual history, the mechanics of systemic racism, media literacy, and the practice of questioning the narrator, including this one. Reynolds tells the reader not to trust him blindly. That instruction is the curriculum.
hook: This is not a history of Black people. This is a history of racist ideas, where they came from, and what they look like now.

title: Stellaluna
author: Janell Cannon
orbital_description: Stellaluna is secretly a bat biology textbook: echolocation, nocturnal behavior, fruit-eating, and bat anatomy appear throughout. Alongside the science, the book explores the cost of belonging and the relief of being exactly who you are.
hook: A young bat falls from her mother's arms and must live as a bird — until she discovers that being herself is better than fitting in.

title: Steve Jobs: The Man Who Thought Different
author: Karen Blumenthal
orbital_description: The book opens Silicon Valley geography and history, the design philosophy behind Apple products, the economics of the personal computer revolution, and the question of whether visionary leadership and decent behavior are compatible.
hook: Steve Jobs changed what a computer looks like and what it feels like to use one — and the biography is honest about what that cost him and the people around him.

title: Still Life
author: Alex London
orbital_description: The book opens art history through the still life tradition from Chardin to Cézanne, visual comedy through the gap between narrator's certainty and illustrated reality, and meta-narrative through a story that comments on its own storytelling. Zelinsky's paintings-within-the-painting are works of art in their own right. This is the funniest 2024 picture book for children who love art.
hook: A painter explains the total predictability of still life — while his painting comes spectacularly, hilariously alive behind him.

title: Stone Soup
author: Jon J Muth
orbital_description: Jon J Muth relocates the European folk tale to China and replaces soldiers with Zen monks. The watercolor style carries Chinese landscape painting traditions while the story carries behavioral economics: how the monks engineer generosity by modeling it. Each villager contributes one ingredient, and the child absorbs nutrition, community dynamics, the psychology of scarcity versus abundance, and the structure of cumulative narratives. The lesson is not about soup. It is about how trust builds when someone goes first.
hook: Three monks arrive in a village where nobody shares. They make soup from a stone. Somehow, everyone eats.

title: Stop That Mop!
author: Jonathan Fenske
orbital_description: The 2026 Geisel Award winner opens cause-and-effect reasoning through escalating comic consequences, the vocabulary of cleaning tools as a context for phonics, and the narrative structure of a problem that compounds. Fenske's visual comedy and precise word choice build reading stamina through genuine laughter.
hook: The mop is out of control, and nobody told it the rules — comedy and cause-and-effect logic for beginning readers.

title: Stuart Little
author: E.B. White
orbital_description: Stuart opens New York City geography, questions of belonging and identity, navigation, and the concept of pursuing something even when you don't know if you'll find it. Stuart's confidence in a world built for bigger creatures is the lesson.
hook: A mouse-child born to a human family sets out across New York to find the bird he loves — and the journey matters more than arriving.

title: Sulwe
author: Lupita Nyong'o
heads_up: Addresses colorism directly. The child character experiences sadness about her skin tone before the resolution.
orbital_description: Lupita Nyong'o wrote this from her own childhood experience with colorism. Sulwe wants to be lighter. Her mother tells her a story about Day and Night, and the book lifts into a cosmological fable where darkness is not the absence of beauty but the canvas it requires. Vashti Harrison's illustrations carry astronomy (star formation, light and shadow) and Kenyan visual culture simultaneously. The child absorbs skin science, self-worth, celestial mechanics, and cultural beauty standards through a single bedtime story.
hook: A girl whose skin is the color of midnight learns that the night sky is where stars live.

title: Summer at Squee
author: Andrea Wang
heads_up: PUB YEAR NEEDS VERIFICATION — Charlotte Huck Honor 2025 = likely 2024 pub.
orbital_description: Charlotte Huck Honor 2025. Andrea Wang (Magic Ramen, ALSC Notable) opens the social-emotional experience of a Chinese American girl navigating identity in a space where she doesn't quite fit any category, and the geography of summer camp as a container for rapid self-discovery. NOTE: May be 2024 pub — verify.
hook: A Chinese American girl has one summer to figure out who she is — at a camp called Squee where nothing goes as expected.

title: Sunday
author: Marcelo Tolentino
orbital_description: The NYT Best Illustrated 2025 winner opens the social-emotional concept of intentional rest, the role of family rituals in belonging, and the contrast between the pace of a Sunday and the rest of the week. Tolentino's art, translated from Brazilian Portuguese, uses color and pacing to physically slow the reader down.
hook: Sunday is the slow day — the day that belongs to the whole family — and this book makes you feel what that rest actually means.

title: Sundust
author: Zeke Peña
orbital_description: The 2026 Caldecott Honor Book opens Southwestern desert ecology through the role of solar energy in a desert ecosystem, the geography of the American Southwest, and the visual physics of light across different times of day. Peña's illustration style draws from his Chicano graphic arts tradition to make the science radiant.
hook: A breathtaking visual journey through the desert where the sun shapes everything it touches — landscape, color, shadow, and life.

title: Swimmy
author: Leo Lionni
orbital_description: The book opens ocean ecosystems, animal defense strategies including schooling and camouflage, and problem-solving through collective action. Swimmy's solution is a real biological strategy used by fish.
hook: A small black fish leads his red school to swim together in the shape of a giant fish — and teaches camouflage and cooperation in one image.

title: Swirl by Swirl: Spirals in Nature
author: Joyce Sidman
orbital_description: Identifies spirals across kingdoms: animal, plant, weather, cosmos.
hook: Spirals hold shells together, unwind fern fronds, shape galaxies, and curl your ear.

title: Sylvester and the Magic Pebble
author: William Steig
orbital_description: The book moves through geology, seasonal ecology, and the psychology of being trapped. The family's grief is as real as any emotion in picture books. The resolution depends on coincidence, love, and presence.
hook: Sylvester finds a magic pebble and turns himself into a rock — and has to wait through every season until his family's grief accidentally saves him.

title: Tanning the Hide
author: Karina Iceberg
orbital_description: PW starred (January 2026 PW new and noteworthy). Debut by Aleut-Alutiiq author Karina Iceberg. Opens the chemistry and biology of hide tanning as a traditional preservation technique, the specific geography and culture of Aleut communities, the concept of community knowledge as the accumulated science of generations, and the importance of beginning with gratitude.
hook: An Aleut-Alutiiq community gathers to tan a large hide by hand — and the step-by-step process reveals both the chemistry of traditional materials and the social architecture of collective work.

title: Teaching for Change: How Septima Clark Led the Civil Rights Movement to Voting Justice
author: Yvonne Clark-Rhines and Monica Clark-Robinson
orbital_description: PW starred. Opens the voting rights movement through the specific mechanism of literacy as a qualification for voting and Septima Clark's Citizenship Schools as the infrastructure that bypassed that barrier, the civil rights geography of the South Carolina Sea Islands, and the concept of education as political power.
hook: Septima Clark taught thousands of Black Southerners to read and write — because in 1960s America, you couldn't register to vote if you couldn't pass a literacy test.

title: Temple Grandin
author: Sy Montgomery
heads_up: Slaughterhouse context. Childhood bullying.
orbital_description: Temple's brain works differently, and that difference is the source of her genius.
hook: Temple Grandin thinks in pictures, not words. That is why she understands cows better than any human alive.

title: The Arabian Nights: Tales of 1001 Nights (Retold for Children)
author: Hanan Al-Shaykh
heads_up: Some tales include violence, deception, and romantic content consistent with the original tradition. This retelling is edited for younger readers but retains moral complexity.
orbital_description: Hanan Al-Shaykh retells the 1001 Nights with the literary sophistication of the original and the accessibility needed for young advanced readers. The nested narrative structure (a story within a story within a story) teaches narrative architecture. The settings carry medieval Islamic geography: Baghdad, Cairo, Damascus, the Indian Ocean trade routes. The child absorbs Middle Eastern literary tradition, Islamic Golden Age culture, moral philosophy through parable, and the idea that storytelling itself is a survival technology. Scheherazade is not a victim. She is a strategist.
hook: Scheherazade tells stories to save her life. Each story contains another story. The structure is the lesson.

title: The Arguers
author: Corinna Luyken
orbital_description: SLJ Best Picture Book 2025. Corinna Luyken (The Book of Mistakes) opens conflict resolution through the structural logic of a fairy tale: two characters argue with such commitment that the argument itself becomes the story. The resolution arrives without moralizing and the visual humor is precise.
hook: A new fairy tale that feels as old as time — about two characters who are relentlessly, hilariously, unstoppably arguing — from one of the most visually distinctive voices in children's books.

title: The Astronaut Who Painted the Moon
author: Dean Robbins
orbital_description: Dean Robbins tells the story of Alan Bean, the Apollo 12 astronaut who became a painter. Bean realized that photographs of the moon were monochrome and flat, but what he saw was textured, colorful, and alive with reflected earthlight. Sean Rubin's illustrations toggle between NASA technical precision and Bean's own impressionistic painting style. The child absorbs lunar geology, the Apollo program, the physics of light on the moon's surface, and the argument that art can document reality in ways that technology cannot. Bean mixed lunar dust into his paint. That detail carries the whole book.
hook: Alan Bean walked on the moon. Then he spent the rest of his life painting what he saw there because no photograph could capture it.

title: The Bard and the Book: How the First Folio Saved the Plays of William Shakespeare from Oblivion
author: Ann Bausum
orbital_description: ALSC 2026 Notable Recordings (implies 2025 book pub). Ann Bausum opens the history of the English Renaissance theater through the specific drama of the First Folio's publication in 1623, the technology of the early printing press, the biography of Shakespeare as a playwright rather than a genius, and the concept of literary preservation as a form of cultural survival.
hook: Eighteen of Shakespeare's plays would have been lost forever — but two actors spent years collecting his manuscripts and publishing the First Folio. This is that story.

title: The Bear Out There
author: Jess Hannigan
orbital_description: The ALSC 2026 Notable Book uses a Goldilocks inversion: a narrator whose total certainty is visually contradicted on every spread. The book teaches perspective-taking, the cost of overconfidence, and visual literacy. The reader knows more than the narrator from the opening page.
hook: A know-it-all girl explains everything about the bear outside — and the reader can see from the very first page that she's completely wrong.

title: The Beetle Book
author: Steve Jenkins
orbital_description: Steve Jenkins uses his signature cut-paper collage to present beetles at actual size, from the titan beetle (6.5 inches) to nearly invisible species. Each spread covers a different adaptation: bioluminescence, chemical defense, camouflage, horn combat, dung rolling. The child absorbs entomology, evolutionary adaptation, comparative anatomy, and global biogeography (beetles from six continents). Jenkins' art technique itself teaches scale, proportion, and the relationship between illustration and scientific communication.
hook: One out of every four living things on Earth is a beetle. Steve Jenkins drew every one of them to scale.

title: The Big Red Barn
author: Margaret Wise Brown
orbital_description: The book maps a farm ecosystem through a single day, naming animals and their sounds and habitats. The day-night cycle anchors the story in the rotation of the earth. Simple language, complete world.
hook: A farm day from morning to night — every animal named, every sound captured, the whole cycle complete.

title: The Biggest Bear
author: Lynd Ward
orbital_description: The book is an early conservation lesson: bear behavior, the ecology of bears in New England, the ethics of keeping wild animals, and the weight of responsibility. The ending is genuinely hard and genuinely right.
hook: Johnny brings home a bear cub — and learns the hard way that wild animals and farms don't mix for long.

title: The Book of Turtles
author: Sy Montgomery
orbital_description: The 2024 Sibert Honor book opens turtle anatomy including the shell as an integral part of the skeleton, evolutionary biology across 220 million years, conservation biology for critically endangered species, and the chemistry of turtle navigation using the earth's magnetic field. Patterson's detailed illustrations are accurate enough to use as field guides.
hook: Turtles are 220 million years old and have survived five mass extinctions — and the science of how they do it is more astonishing than the number.

title: The Book With No Pictures
author: B.J. Novak
orbital_description: The book teaches that language creates images; that reading aloud is performative; that words have power over the speaker. Children discover they can make an adult say almost anything if it is written in a book.
hook: A book that forces adults to say increasingly ridiculous things out loud — and proves that words alone are entertainment.

title: The Boy Who Harnessed the Wind (Young Readers Edition)
author: William Kamkwamba & Bryan Mealer
heads_up: Famine with real consequences.
orbital_description: The picture book gives the outline. This gives the weight. Physics of electromagnetic induction explained through his own learning.
hook: The full story of how a boy who could not afford school taught himself enough physics to save his village.

title: The Boys Who Challenged Hitler: Knud Pedersen and the Churchill Club
author: Phillip Hoose
orbital_description: Sibert Honor 2016. Opens the history of the Danish resistance to Nazi occupation through the specific activities and capture of teenage resisters, the chemistry of sabotage, the geography of occupied Denmark, and the concept of teenage agency in historical events through Knud Pedersen's own memoir-based account.
hook: In 1941, Danish teenagers formed the Churchill Club, sabotaged German vehicles and supply depots, got arrested, and kept resisting from prison.

title: The Brilliant Deep: Rebuilding the World's Coral Reefs
author: Kate Messner
orbital_description: Opens the marine biology of coral through the specific biology of coral polyps (animals, not plants), the chemistry of ocean acidification and its effect on calcium carbonate shell-building, the conservation science of coral reef restoration including the fragment cultivation technique Nedimyer developed, and the geography of the Florida Keys as a fragile marine ecosystem.
hook: Ken Nedimyer decided to regrow the coral reef off the Florida Keys by hand, one fragment at a time — and the science of rebuilding a living ecosystem from scratch is extraordinary.

title: The Bug Girl
author: Benjamin Harper & Sarah Glenn Marsh
heads_up: Depicts bullying related to a child's interests. Resolved positively.
orbital_description: Based on the real Sophia Spencer, a girl who was bullied for loving insects and whose mother wrote to the Entomological Society of America for help. The society responded with thousands of letters from scientists worldwide. The book carries insect anatomy, species identification, and the professional field of entomology while telling the story of a child whose obsession was validated by the very community that shares it. The child absorbs insect biology, scientific community structures, and the principle that the thing that makes you strange might make you a scientist.
hook: Sophia loved bugs. The other kids thought that was weird. Then an entomologist wrote back.

title: The Camel Library: A True Story from Pakistan
author: Marzieh Abbas
orbital_description: SLJ June 2025 starred review. Opens the geography and rural life of Pakistan including the specific landscape where the camel library travels, the concept of information access as a human right and the history of traveling libraries worldwide, and the economics of literacy in communities without permanent institutions.
hook: In rural Pakistan, a camel carries a library from village to village — and the camel librarian has been bringing books to children who have no other access to them for years.

title: The Carrot Seed
author: Ruth Krauss
orbital_description: Four words on most pages, one enormous scientific lesson: seeds need time, specific conditions, and unwavering care. The family's skepticism models social pressure against perseverance; the carrot validates patient scientific process.
hook: A boy plants a carrot seed while everyone tells him it won't grow — and it grows.

title: The Cecilia Payne Story: Reaching for the Stars
author: Laura Alary
heads_up: TITLE NEEDS VERIFICATION before Supabase import — working title only, may differ from published title.
orbital_description: PW starred (January 2026 PW new and noteworthy). Opens stellar astrophysics through Payne's 1925 discovery that hydrogen and helium are the dominant elements in stars, the history of Harvard's women astronomers who did the foundational work of modern astronomy while earning almost nothing, and the institutional erasure of women's scientific contributions.
hook: Cecilia Payne discovered that stars are made mostly of hydrogen — and when she published her findings, the male scientist who reviewed her work told her she was wrong. He later admitted she was right and published it as his own discovery.

title: The City of Ember
author: Jeanne DuPrau
orbital_description: Opens the physics of electrical systems through Ember's failing generators, the concept of a closed social system running out of resources, the engineering challenge of a self-sustaining underground environment, and the philosophy of what a society does when it discovers it is not everything it believed it was.
hook: Ember is the only city in the world — it has always been the only city — and its lights are failing. Two teenagers figure out why and what lies beyond the darkness.

title: The Clock
author: Pam Fong
orbital_description: ALSC 2026 Notable. Opens the history of the Gare d'Orsay as a train station converted to one of the world's great art museums, the craft of clock restoration and horological history, the geography of Paris's cultural institutions, and the concept of preservation as a form of cultural memory.
hook: The beloved clock of the Gare d'Orsay train station in Paris was lost for decades — until the station became the Musée d'Orsay and the clock was restored to its former glory.

title: The Cricket in Times Square
author: George Selden
orbital_description: The book opens cricket biology including stridulation and molting, New York City geography through the subway system and Times Square, the economics of a small family business, and classical music through a cricket's perfect ear.
hook: A Connecticut cricket ends up in the Times Square subway station and discovers he can play music perfectly from memory — a gift that saves a family's newsstand.

title: The Dark Lord of Derkholm
author: Diana Wynne Jones
orbital_description: Carnegie Medal. Opens the economics of cultural tourism through the satirical lens of a fantasy world treated as an amusement park, the structure of epic fantasy as a genre through its systematic parody, the ecology of a fantasy world as a real functioning ecosystem, and the concept of what happens when a culture is commodified for outsiders' entertainment.
hook: A demon tourist company brings busloads of tourists from another world to see a real Dark Lord and a quest — and it is absolutely destroying the place.

title: The Darkest Dark
author: Chris Hadfield
orbital_description: Astronaut Chris Hadfield wrote this from his own childhood: a boy terrified of darkness who watched Apollo 11 on television in 1969 and reframed darkness from threat to frontier. The Fan Brothers' illustrations carry the actual mechanics of the moon landing, mission control, lunar geography, and the rocket staging process while the story carries the psychology of fear transformation. The child absorbs space science, Cold War history, aerospace engineering, and the idea that the thing you fear most might be the thing that defines your life.
hook: Chris Hadfield was afraid of the dark. Then he watched the moon landing and realized that space is the darkest dark there is.

title: The Daughter of Auschwitz: The Girl Who Lived to Tell Her Story (Young Readers Edition)
author: Tova Friedman
heads_up: Depicts Holocaust atrocities; preview before reading with younger students. Discuss with care.
orbital_description: SLJ 2025 Stars So Far starred review ('masterfully and beautifully written'). Young readers edition of the adult memoir. Opens the history of the Holocaust through the specific experience of a child survivor, the geography of Auschwitz-Birkenau and the Nazi camp system, and the concept of testimony as a form of historical obligation.
hook: Tova Friedman was six years old when she entered Auschwitz — and she survived to tell a story that the world must not forget.

title: The Day the Books Disappeared
author: Joanna Ho
orbital_description: The 2026 ALSC Notable Book opens the concept of literary diversity as an ecosystem — when one type of book dominates and all others disappear, something essential is lost — alongside the philosophy of reading as perspective expansion, and a gentle age-appropriate introduction to the concept of censorship.
hook: Arnold loves his book so much he wishes all other books would disappear — and when they do, he learns exactly what he's lost.

title: The Day the Crayons Quit
author: Drew Daywalt
orbital_description: Each crayon's complaint is a mini-essay on color psychology: red is overworked, white is overlooked, blue is asked to color outside water. The letters model persuasive writing; the colors model perspective-taking.
hook: Duncan's crayons each have a grievance — and their letters reveal color theory, fair use, and the full spectrum of artistic frustration.

title: The Doomsday Detectives: How Walter and Luis Alvarez Solved the Mystery of Dinosaur Extinction
author: Cindy Jenson-Elliott
orbital_description: SLJ 2025 Stars So Far starred review. Opens the history of the K-Pg extinction event through the specific detective work of Walter and Luis Alvarez — Nobel Prize-winner Luis Alvarez and his geologist son Walter — who found the iridium anomaly in the rock layers and connected it to a massive asteroid impact. The book models scientific argument as a historical process.
hook: For decades scientists argued about what killed the dinosaurs — until a father-son team found a thin layer of iridium at the exact point in the geological record where dinosaurs disappeared, and proved an asteroid did it.

title: The Dot
author: Peter H. Reynolds
orbital_description: The shortest persuasive essay on creative courage ever written. Creative process and color theory.
hook: A girl who says she cannot draw makes one angry dot on a blank page, and her teacher frames it.

title: The Dot
author: Peter H. Reynolds
orbital_description: Vashti cannot draw. Her teacher says: make a mark and see where it takes you. She jabs a dot. The teacher frames it. What follows is an arc through color theory, artistic confidence, mentorship dynamics, and the moment a child discovers that creation does not require permission or perfection. Reynolds' watercolors carry the visual art curriculum while the story carries the emotional one. The last page passes the torch to a younger child.
hook: A girl who says she cannot draw makes a single dot. Then everything changes.

title: The Dream Builder's Blueprint: Dr. King's Message to Young People
author: Alice Faye Duncan
orbital_description: PW starred. Opens the concept of erasure poetry as a literary technique, the history of Dr. King's lesser-known 1967 address to South Philadelphia students, the civil rights movement's philosophy of nonviolent protest, and the specific words King used when speaking directly to children and teenagers about their own role in history.
hook: An erasure poem built from Dr. King's 1967 speech to teenagers at a Philadelphia school — finding the message to young people that has been inside his words all along.

title: The Enigma Girls: How Ten Teenagers Broke Ciphers, Kept Secrets, and Helped Win World War II
author: Candace Fleming
orbital_description: The book opens the mathematics of code-breaking including frequency analysis and the Enigma machine's structure, the geography of Bletchley Park as an operational intelligence center, and the specific secrecy required of the Wrens — women who knew one of the most important secrets of the war and maintained it for three decades.
hook: Ten teenage girls were recruited to break Nazi codes at Bletchley Park — and they couldn't tell anyone, including their families, for 30 years.

title: The Evolution of Calpurnia Tate
author: Jacqueline Kelly
orbital_description: The collision between Victorian gender expectations and scientific inquiry. Newbery Honor.
hook: In 1899 Texas, a girl discovers Darwin's Origin of Species and realizes the world she is being prepared for and the world she lives in are two different species.

title: The Eyes and the Impossible
author: Dave Eggers
orbital_description: The 2024 Newbery Medal winner opens urban park ecology, the philosophy of freedom and community obligation, and the specific gift of noticing the world with the attention of a creature whose survival depends on it. Harris's bold block-print illustrations give the book the visual energy of a dog running at full speed.
hook: Johannes is a free dog who sees everything in the urban park he guards — and what he sees is about to change everything.

title: The Fabulous Fannie Farmer: The Woman Who Changed the Way America Cooks
author: Emma Bland Smith
orbital_description: The 2025 Best STEM Book winner opens the chemistry of cooking through standardized measurement, the history of domestic science as a legitimate discipline, Boston geography and the cooking school tradition, and the biography of a woman who turned cooking into a science by insisting on precision. The recipes she standardized are in the back.
hook: Fannie Farmer invented standardized measurements — before her, a pinch was a pinch and nobody's baking came out the same way twice.

title: The Fantastic Undersea Life of Jacques Cousteau
author: Dan Yaccarino
orbital_description: Biography, marine biology, French history, engineering, and conservation activism.
hook: A sickly French boy who could barely swim invented the technology that let humans breathe underwater.

title: The First State of Being
author: Erin Entrada Kelly
orbital_description: The 2025 Newbery Medal winner opens time travel physics through accessible paradox, the history of the Y2K moment and what people feared about the millennium, apartment complex community as microcosm, and the specific weight of questions that have no answer. The 1999 setting is rendered with documentary accuracy.
hook: A boy living in a 1999 apartment complex meets a time traveler from the future — and the questions they ask each other are the ones that matter most.

title: The Five Sides of Marjorie Rice: How an Amateur Mathematician Discovered New Shapes
author: Amy Alznauer
orbital_description: ALSC 2026 Notable, Middle category. Opens the mathematics of tessellation through the geometry of pentagons, the history of a mathematical problem that had been considered solved in 1918 and was reopened by an amateur in 1975, and the concept that significant mathematical discovery is not the exclusive domain of credentialed professionals.
hook: Marjorie Rice was a homemaker with no formal mathematical training who discovered four new types of pentagon tessellation that professional mathematicians had missed.

title: The Fourteenth Goldfish
author: Jennifer L. Holm
orbital_description: Comedy premise opening into real science: the immortal jellyfish, telomeres, cellular senescence, and ethics of anti-aging.
hook: Ellie's grandfather discovers the secret to reversing aging and turns himself into a teenager.

title: The Friendship Train: A True Story of Helping and Healing After World War II
author: Debbie Levy
orbital_description: Confirmed June 3, 2025. Opens the geography of post-WWII Europe through the specific history of the Friendship Train — an American humanitarian initiative that crossed the country collecting food before crossing the Atlantic — and the French reciprocal train that carried wine, perfume, and heartfelt gifts back. A history of generosity as diplomacy.
hook: In 1947, Americans loaded a train with 700 boxcars of food and sent it to starving families in France and Italy — and the French sent a Friendship Train back, full of gifts and gratitude.

title: The Garden of Abdul Gasazi
author: Chris Van Allsburg
orbital_description: The book introduces unreliable narration and ambiguity: did the magician turn the dog into a duck or not? Van Allsburg leaves the answer open, teaching children to tolerate uncertainty and construct their own interpretations.
hook: A boy chases a dog into a forbidden garden — and comes out unsure of what is real.

title: The Girl Who Drew Butterflies
author: Joyce Sidman
orbital_description: Maria Sibylla Merian documented insect metamorphosis decades before the scientific establishment accepted it. Sibert Honor.
hook: In 1679, when people believed insects were born from mud, a German woman proved them wrong by painting.

title: The Girl Who Drew Butterflies
author: Joyce Sidman
orbital_description: Joyce Sidman profiles Maria Sibylla Merian, the 17th-century naturalist who documented insect metamorphosis through meticulous illustration when the prevailing theory held that insects arose from spontaneous generation. The book reproduces Merian's actual paintings alongside the biography, so the child absorbs entomology, the history of scientific illustration, the scientific method in its earliest form, colonial-era Suriname, and the systemic exclusion of women from institutional science. Merian funded her own expedition. She published her own work. She was right.
hook: In 1679, a woman sailed to Suriname to draw insects when the scientific establishment said they were born from mud.

title: The Girl Who Thought in Pictures
author: Julia Finley Mosca
heads_up: Depicts childhood struggles with communication and sensory overload. Handled with respect.
orbital_description: Julia Finley Mosca tells Temple Grandin's story in rhyming verse: the nonverbal childhood, the aunt's ranch where animals made sense when people did not, the PhD in animal science, the revolutionary livestock handling systems she designed because she could see the world the way a cow sees it. Daniel Rieley's illustrations carry the engineering of Grandin's curved chute designs and the neuroscience of visual thinking. The child absorbs autism as a cognitive difference (not a deficit), animal behavior science, agricultural engineering, and the principle that the way you think differently might be the exact thing the world needs.
hook: Temple Grandin thinks in pictures. The doctors said it was a disability. The cows said it was exactly what they needed.

title: The Giving Tree
author: Shel Silverstein
orbital_description: The apple, branches, and trunk map a real tree's anatomy; the seasons track the boy's aging; the moral ambiguity about whether the tree is wise or the boy is selfish makes this picture book a conversation engine across decades.
hook: A tree gives everything she has to a boy she loves across a lifetime — and the book refuses to tell you how to feel about it.

title: The Good Egg
author: Jory John
orbital_description: Jory John personifies a good egg who does everything right: cleans up, helps everyone, holds it together while the other eggs are rotten. He cracks from the effort. Pete Oswald's illustrations carry the physical metaphor (the crack, the bandage, the slow repair) while the text carries the psychology of perfectionism, people-pleasing, and burnout. The resolution is not selfishness. It is boundaries. The child absorbs emotional vocabulary for burnout, the difference between helping and overextending, and the biology of eggs as a surprisingly effective metaphor for structural integrity under stress.
hook: He was a good egg. He was such a good egg that he cracked.

title: The Great Brain
author: John D. Fitzgerald
orbital_description: The book opens 1890s Utah territorial history, small-town economics, the ethics of cleverness used for profit, and the specific texture of a Mormon pioneer community. Tom's schemes are morally complex and genuinely clever.
hook: Tom Fitzgerald's brain is so great he can figure out how to make money from anything — and his schemes are the education of an entire Utah mining town in 1896.

title: The Great Fire
author: Jim Murphy
orbital_description: Newbery Honor 1996. Opens the chemistry of fire and fire propagation through the specific conditions that made Chicago's wooden city so combustible, the history of urban planning through the rebuilding decisions that made Chicago the architectural capital of America, and the social history of who lost everything vs. who could afford to rebuild.
hook: The Great Chicago Fire of 1871 burned for two days, destroyed 17,000 buildings, and killed 300 people — and how it started is only the beginning of what the fire reveals.

title: The Great Kapok Tree
author: Lynne Cherry
orbital_description: Lynne Cherry sends a man with an axe into the Amazon and then gives voice to every animal that depends on the kapok tree: the boa constrictor, the bee, the toucan, the jaguar, the tree frog, the child from the Yanomami tribe. Each creature teaches a different layer of the rainforest ecosystem while making a persuasive argument. The child absorbs tropical ecology, canopy stratification, carbon cycles, Indigenous land rights, and the structure of persuasive rhetoric in a single read-aloud. The man wakes up. His decision is the last page.
hook: A man enters the rainforest to chop down a tree. While he sleeps, every creature in the canopy makes its case.

title: The Great Lakes: Our Freshwater Treasure
author: Barb Rosenstock
orbital_description: Two starred reviews (Booklist, SLJ) for 2025 publication. Barb Rosenstock (The Noisy Paint Box, Caldecott Honor) opens the hydrology and geology of the Great Lakes as a single connected freshwater system, the Anishinaabe cultural history of the region, the ecology of 3,500 species that depend on the lakes, and the environmental history of pollution and recovery.
hook: The Great Lakes hold 21% of the world's surface fresh water — and this book takes readers through their geological formation 10,000 years ago to the Anishinaabe nations who lived there first to the conservation crises of today.

title: The Great Wall of Lucy Wu
author: Wendy Wan-Long Shang
orbital_description: Chinese-American identity through a girl who wants to be normal and a grandmother who refuses to let her forget.
hook: Lucy Wu has her year planned until her Chinese grandmother moves in and ruins everything by being completely right.

title: The Hair Book
author: Andrea J. Brown-Wood
heads_up: TITLE needs verification — working title 'The Hair Book'; confirmed Kirkus starred, authors Andrea J. Brown-Wood / Christian Robinson.
orbital_description: Kirkus starred ('a testament to Black beauty destined to be a classic') from Red Fox Literary 2026. Andrea J. Brown-Wood and Christian Robinson (Caldecott Honor) open the cultural and historical significance of Black hair as identity, the inheritance of hair traditions across generations, and the social-emotional concept of belonging rooted in visible heritage.
hook: A celebration of Black hair through generations — the verse goes straight to the heart and the art is a loving archive of Black beauty across time.

title: The Hello Goodbye Window
author: Norton Juster
orbital_description: The book is a love letter to the grandparent-grandchild relationship and to finding magic in the ordinary: a window, a garden, a hug. It teaches children to notice what they already have.
hook: The kitchen window at Nanna and Poppy's house is where everything happens — hello, goodbye, and all the in-between.

title: The History of We
author: Nikkolas Smith
orbital_description: Kirkus Best of 2025 opens the history of human origins in Africa including cave paintings, early musical instruments, and scientific innovations, the concept of all human history as shared history, and the specific contribution of African civilizations to global culture. Smith's paintings render history as living and personal.
hook: Human history began on the African continent — and this book pays tribute to the innovations of human cultures from the very beginning, with spellbinding verse and startlingly intimate paintings.

title: The Hive Detectives: Chronicle of a Honey Bee Catastrophe
author: Loree Griffin Burns
orbital_description: Sibert Honor 2011. Opens the biology of honeybees including the social organization of the hive through caste roles (queen, workers, drones), the ecology of pollination as the mechanism connecting bees to the entire food system, the scientific investigation process through the multiple research teams pursuing different hypotheses for Colony Collapse Disorder, and the concept of a sentinel species as an indicator of ecosystem health.
hook: In 2006, beekeepers started finding their hives empty — millions of bees vanishing overnight — and scientists had no idea why.

title: The Honey Bee
author: Kirsten Hall
orbital_description: Kirsten Hall wrote this in verse that moves like a bee: short lines, rhythmic pulse, forward momentum. Isabelle Arsenault's illustrations layer botany into every spread without a single diagram. The child absorbs pollination, seasonal cycles, colony structure, and the economics of honey production through rhythm alone. After this one, she will crouch next to every bee in the yard and watch it work.
hook: A poem that hums. Your child will never look at a flower the same way after this one.

title: The Hundred Dresses
author: Eleanor Estes
orbital_description: Eleanor Estes published this in 1944 and it has not dated. Wanda Petronski, a Polish-American girl in a Connecticut school, claims to have a hundred dresses. The other girls mock her daily. When Wanda leaves, she donates a hundred drawings of dresses, each one extraordinary. The narrator, Maddie, is not the bully. She is the bystander, which is worse, and Estes knows it. The child absorbs the social dynamics of exclusion, the role of the bystander, the relationship between poverty and art, immigrant identity, and the permanent weight of regret for things you did not say.
hook: Wanda says she has a hundred dresses at home. Everyone laughs. Nobody looks at the drawings.

title: The Iguanodon's Horn: How Two Centuries of Mystery, Mistakes, and Fossil Fixes Changed the Way We Look at Dinosaurs
author: Sean Rubin
orbital_description: The Kirkus Best of 2024 book opens the history of paleontology through the Iguanodon as a case study in scientific revision: the first fossil was found in 1822, and scientists kept revising their understanding of the animal for 200 years. Every revision is a lesson in hypothesis, evidence, and the scientific method as a self-correcting process.
hook: Scientists put the Iguanodon's horn on its nose for 130 years — and every correction reveals something true about how science actually works.

title: The Impossible Creatures
author: Katherine Rundell
orbital_description: The book builds its fantasy world on ecological logic: the Archipelago functions as a real wildlife habitat, its creatures follow internally consistent behavioral rules, and the threat they face mirrors real conservation biology. The world-building is built to make children think about why ecosystems matter.
hook: A boy discovers that mythological creatures are real and living in a secret archipelago — and they are dying.

title: The Incredibly Human Henson Blayze
author: Derrick Barnes
orbital_description: The Coretta Scott King Award-winning novel opens identity formation through the specific lens of a Black boy navigating the question of what 'incredible' means when the world has often said it doesn't apply to people who look like him. Barnes's signature voice celebrates Black joy and humanity without making suffering the central story.
hook: Henson Blayze is discovering who he is — and the book insists that being human is the most remarkable thing about him.

title: The Ink Witch
author: Steph Cherrywell
orbital_description: PW starred + Kirkus starred (2025). Opens the concept of art-making as a form of magic through the visual metaphor of sentient ink, the history of illustration as craft, and the social-emotional arc of a young person discovering that their creative power is greater and stranger than they were taught. The graphic novel format makes the ink's behavior visible in ways prose cannot.
hook: A young witch discovers that the ink she uses for spells has a life of its own — and the discovery reshapes everything she thought she knew about magic and art.

title: The Invisible Parade
author: Leigh Bardugo and John Picacio
orbital_description: The 2026 Belpré Illustration Honor Book opens the history of Mardi Gras as a specifically New Orleans cultural tradition, the African American roots of second-line parade culture, the geography of New Orleans, and the concept of ancestor memory in community celebration.
hook: Every year, an invisible parade walks alongside the Mardi Gras parade in New Orleans — made of everyone who came before.

title: The Iridescence of Birds
author: Patricia MacLachlan
orbital_description: Patricia MacLachlan imagines Matisse's childhood: the gray northern French town, the pigeons whose neck feathers held iridescent color, the weaver father whose looms taught pattern, the seed-shop mother whose displays taught arrangement. Hadley Hooper's printmaking illustrations deliberately echo the flat, bold color planes Matisse would later make famous. The child absorbs color theory, the physics of iridescence, French geography, textile arts, and the biographical truth that most artists' styles originate in what they noticed as children.
hook: Before Henri Matisse was Matisse, he was a boy who watched pigeons and saw every color the world was hiding.

title: The Keeping Quilt
author: Patricia Polacco
orbital_description: The quilt is the medium for teaching textile arts, immigration history, memory transmission, and the physical continuity of family. Each generation adds to the story the quilt holds; children begin to understand how objects carry history.
hook: A quilt made from immigrant clothing travels through four generations — a family history you can touch.

title: The Last Kids on Earth
author: Max Brallier
orbital_description: Opens the horror/comedy genre through inversion of apocalypse as liberation rather than tragedy, zombie science as a cognitive science question, the sociology of a suburban landscape reclaimed by nature, and the engineering problem of fortifying a tree house against monsters. The humor is the delivery system.
hook: Jack Sullivan lives alone in his tree house after the zombie apocalypse with a monster pet and a baseball bat — and this is the greatest time of his life.

title: The Last Mapmaker
author: Christina Soontornvat
orbital_description: The 2023 Newbery Honor book opens the history and science of cartography including map projections and the politics of what gets included, maritime navigation, Southeast Asian geography and mythology, and the class dynamics of who gets to go on expeditions. Soontornvat's Southeast Asian-inspired world is built from genuine regional history.
hook: An apprentice to the kingdom's mapmaker joins a sea voyage and discovers that the maps — and the mapmaker — have been lying.

title: The Last Stand
author: Antwan Eady
heads_up: PUB YEAR NEEDS VERIFICATION — Charlotte Huck Award 2025 = likely 2024 pub.
orbital_description: Charlotte Huck Award Winner 2025. Antwan Eady (Nigel and the Moon) opens the economic history of Black-owned businesses in America through the specific geography of a neighborhood grocery store, the concept of community commerce as cultural preservation, and the social-emotional weight of a business that is also a community institution. NOTE: May be 2024 pub — verify.
hook: A family's Black-owned grocery store is making its last stand against a big chain moving in — and the store holds 100 years of community history in its shelves.

title: The Lemonade War
author: Jacqueline Davies
orbital_description: Every business decision is real: location, pricing, marketing, profit margin, sunk costs, cash flow. The sibling dynamic is emotionally accurate. The book ends with a ledger that proves both children right about different things.
hook: Brother and sister run competing lemonade stands the week before school starts — and the competition is also a real economics textbook.

title: The Library in the Woods
author: R. Gregory Christie
orbital_description: The Coretta Scott King Award book opens the history of Black Americans' relationship with literacy — from reading bans under slavery through the creation of community libraries — the concept of library access as a civil right, and the social-emotional importance of a community that invests in its own children's minds.
hook: Deep in the woods where there was no library, a Black community built one — and every book in it was a declaration.

title: The Lighthouse Keeper
author: Eugenio Fernández Vázquez
orbital_description: The NYT Best Illustrated 2025 winner opens the technology of lighthouses as navigation systems, the geography of coastal sea routes, and the social-emotional concept of meaningful solitude — doing important work that no one watches. Villanueva Segovia's art earned the NYT judges' highest distinction. Translated from Spanish by Kit Maude.
hook: A lighthouse keeper and the sailors his light guides — a meditation on purpose and solitude that has astonishing visual depth.

title: The Lions' Run
author: Sara Pennypacker
heads_up: Wartime violence, kidnapping of children; discuss before reading.
orbital_description: Instant New York Times Bestseller. Four starred reviews (Booklist, Horn Book, PW, Kirkus). Sara Pennypacker (Pax) opens the Nazi Lebensborn program — the kidnapping of children from conquered countries to Aryanize them — a corner of WWII history almost never covered in MG. Opens French geography under occupation, the structure of the Resistance, and the specific courage of an eleven-year-old courier. Pub Feb 3, 2026.
hook: Lucas is a French orphan in a Nazi-occupied village, known for being a coward — and the kittens he rescues lead him directly into the Resistance and the Lebensborn program's stolen children.

title: The Listening Walk
author: Paul Showers
orbital_description: A science book about sound waves disguised as a walk around the block.
hook: A girl and her father walk through the neighborhood without talking, and the world gets incredibly loud.

title: The Little House
author: Virginia Lee Burton
orbital_description: Compressed into one picture book: urban growth, seasonal cycles, environmental change, transportation history, and the passage of time across generations. The illustrations map a century of American city-building.
hook: A little house watches the city grow up around her across a century — and finally finds her way back to the countryside.

title: The Longitude Prize
author: Joan Dash
orbital_description: Newbery Honor 2001. Opens the physics of celestial navigation through the mathematical relationship between time and longitude (15 degrees per hour of time difference from Greenwich), the history of 18th-century maritime disasters caused by inability to determine longitude, the mechanics of clockmaking through Harrison's H-1 through H-4 chronometers, and the sociology of scientific institutions and their resistance to solutions from outside the establishment.
hook: For centuries, sailors could not determine their longitude at sea — and ships wrecked by the thousands. A clockmaker named John Harrison built the solution, and the scientific establishment spent decades refusing to accept it.

title: The Lorax
author: Dr. Seuss
orbital_description: This is systems thinking for four-year-olds: the Truffula forest is a real ecosystem model; the thneeds represent products with downstream environmental costs; the Lorax models environmental advocacy; the ending places agency squarely on the child reading the book.
hook: The Once-ler builds an industry that destroys an ecosystem — and a small orange creature tries to stop him one tree at a time.

title: The Lost City of Z (Young Readers Edition)
author: David Grann
heads_up: Explorer disappearance. Colonial attitudes.
orbital_description: Percy Fawcett's obsession. The twist: he was right about the Amazonian civilization.
hook: A British explorer searched the Amazon for a city that scientists said could not exist. He disappeared. Then archaeologists found it.

title: The Lumbering Giants of Windy Pines
author: Mo Netz
orbital_description: The Kirkus Best of 2024 novel opens forest ecology through the specific biology of old-growth woodland, disability and accessibility as lived experience rather than narrative obstacle, and the mystery structure of a community secret kept for decades. The protagonist's wheelchair is a fact, not a plot device.
hook: A girl who uses a wheelchair moves to a motel near terrifying woods — and becomes the only one brave enough to go in.

title: The Many Assassinations of Samir, the Seller of Dreams
author: Daniel Nayeri
